revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:ふとん太鼓]]
{{Wikify|date=July 2022}} [[File:堺型ふとん太鼓 西湊.jpg|thumb|300x300px|{{ill|Sakai-style Futon Taiko|ja|堺型ふとん太鼓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakai-style Futon Taiko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Photo taken at the Nishiminato Taiko Platform in {{ill|Sakai|en|Sakai|qid=Q193428|lt=Sakai City|1=Sakai}}, {{ill|Sakai-ku, Sakai|en|Sakai-ku, Sakai|qid=Q1042008|lt=Sakai-ku|1=Sakai-ku, Sakai}}, {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|qid=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture|1=Osaka Prefecture}} – approximately 1.7 tons). The binding cords at the four corners of the futon are said to represent billowing clouds<ref>[http://www.kobe-du.ac.jp/wp-content/uploads/2009/11/003.pdf]{{ill|Connecting Asia with Taiko Platforms|ja|太鼓台でアジアを結ぶ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Connecting Asia with Taiko Platforms|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} by Kohei Sugiura, {{ill|Kobe Design University|en|Kobe Design University|qid=Q6424307|lt=Kobe Design University|1=Kobe Design University}}, 2008</ref>]] [[File:Futon daiko.jpg|thumbnail|300px|{{ill|Namba Shrine|ja|難波神社|1=Nanba Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Namba Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanba Shrine|qq=}}’s futon taiko. From the 18th-century {{ill|Settsu Meisho Zukai|ja|摂津名所図会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Settsu Meisho Zukai|1=Settsu Meisho Zue|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11498549}}. The child drummer—who becomes the striking participant—wears a conical headscarf, and vendors under shop eaves offer watermelon to the carriers. The caption explains that the drum’s sound is as thunderous as lightning and far exceeds that of the Korean (or Chinese) taiko reputed in the region.]] '''{{nihongo|Futon Daiko|ふとん太鼓}}''' are the large taiko platforms carried in festivals in the {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|qid=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture|1=Osaka Prefecture}}, especially in the former provinces of {{ill|Kawachi Province|en|Kawachi Province|qid=Q1047150|lt=Kawachi|1=Kawachi Province}} and {{ill|Izumi Province|en|Izumi Province|qid=Q1047117|lt=Izumi|1=Izumi Province}}, as well as in and around {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|qid=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}} (including the former provinces of {{ill|Harima Province|en|Harima Province|qid=Q1047523|lt=Harima|1=Harima Province}} and {{ill|Awaji Province|en|Awaji Province|qid=Q1053717|lt=Awaji|1=Awaji Province}}). They are a type of festival float ({{ill|Matsuri float|en|Matsuri float|qid=Q11470974|lt=Yamadashi|1=Matsuri float}}) whose most striking feature is that a huge square futon (bedding) is stacked in an inverted-pyramid shape at what corresponds to its roof. They are also called futon danjiri, futon mikoshi, or simply futon floats.
== Format and Characteristics == *Common Features **Inside each futon taiko the central element is a taiko (drum) over which a group of youth, known as “{{ill|rider|ja|乗り子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/rider|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}” (typically 4–8 in number), is seated. In some districts the riders are tied securely so that even in the midst of vigorous movement they do not fall off. The riders usually wear stage‐style heavy makeup ({{ill|thick ceremonial makeup|ja|厚化粧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/thick ceremonial makeup|1=thick ceremonial makeup|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11409220}}) and lavish costumes. They often use a club that is thick and short—resembling a shortened baseball bat—for striking.
*Osaka Type **This type is characterized by futons set at a relatively small angle and with carvings as the central decorative element. Carvings are also present on the {{ill|door panels|ja|土呂板|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/door panels|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|railings|ja|欄干|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/railings|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 橋|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 橋|1=Bridge|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q12280}}. Among these, some examples known as “{{ill|Yudai Taiko|ja|雄太鼓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yudai Taiko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}” are noted for their simplicity. A notable structural feature is the presence of a “{{ill|tagaya|ja|タガヤ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tagaya|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}”—a series of thin boards layered under the futon, arranged beneath a five-tiered section.
*Awaji Type **On {{ill|Awaji Island|en|Awaji Island|qid=Q504373|lt=Awaji Island|1=Awaji Island}} these are referred to as either “{{ill|futon danjiri|ja|ふとんだんじり|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/futon danjiri|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}” or “{{ill|kaki(gi) danjiri|ja|かき（ぎ）だんじり|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kaki(gi) danjiri|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}”. Although no one has catalogued every surviving example on the island, it is estimated that roughly 200 exist. For ease of movement during festivals, wheels are attached to the base. In southern Awaji the wheels are primarily used to rock the platform like a seesaw or to enable simple rotation; in some areas a rudder ({{ill|Rudder|en|Rudder|qid=Q209760|lt=teji|1=Rudder}} or 梃子) is attached to facilitate handling during rotation or towing. In many cases the offering to the shrine is conceived as a pair with {{ill|Jōruri (music)|en|Jōruri (music)|qid=Q1361891|lt=jōruri|1=Jōruri (music)}}. Local youth repeatedly rehearse prior to the festival and often perform futon taiko songs. In northern Awaji, by contrast, the wheels are removed during the parade ({{ill|miyairi|ja|宮入|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/miyairi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神幸祭|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神幸祭|1=Shinko-shiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q123907414}}) so that the platform is carried; here the emphasis is on the martial grandeur and competitive spirit of the carrying.
*Sakai Type :In the Sakai type, the entire futon taiko is elaborately carved with depictions of mythology, human sentiment, landscapes, and flora and fauna; carvings extend onto the door panels and railings. Here the futon’s thickness increases from the bottom upward and the angle is relatively small; moreover, instead of a golden rope, a material such as {{ill|Rasha|ja|羅紗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rasha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ラシャ|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ラシャ|1=Rasha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11346737}} is used for binding. Its most prominent feature is that beneath the futon a futon platform with a small roof is attached, and even the supporting pillars—extending fully to the ground—are made from a single continuous piece of wood (four integrated columns).
*Shell Mound Type :In this variation, in place of the decorative knots or dragonfly motifs, an element known as “{{ill|mara|ja|魔羅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mara|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 魔|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 魔|1=māra|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q15726943}}” stands upright. The base shows intricate carvings reminiscent of those on the base wheels, and the pillars can slide vertically—a feature known as “{{ill|seriage|ja|せり上げ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/seriage|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.”
*Mountain-type Futon Floats :These are common in areas such as {{ill|Takasago, Hyōgo|en|Takasago, Hyōgo|qid=Q869037|lt=Takasago City|1=Takasago, Hyōgo}}, the northern parts of {{ill|Kakogawa, Hyōgo|en|Kakogawa, Hyōgo|qid=Q750569|lt=Kakogawa City|1=Kakogawa, Hyōgo}}, and the northern region of the former {{ill|Harima|en|Harima|qid=Q3783364|lt=Harima|1=Harima}}. {{Main|Autumn Festivals of Harushū}}
== Futon Taiko in Higashiosaka City == [[File:Futondaiko Ishikiri Osaka JPN 002.jpg|thumbnail|{{ill|Futon Taiko in Higashiosaka|ja|東大阪市のふとん太鼓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futon Taiko in Higashiosaka|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}]] [[File:Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine Mikoshi.JPG|thumb|A mikoshi featuring a futon taiko. Image from the 18th-century {{ill|Settsu Meisho Zukai|ja|摂津名所図会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Settsu Meisho Zukai|1=Settsu Meisho Zue|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11498549}} at {{ill|Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine (Hyōgo)|en|Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine (Hyōgo)|qid=Q3594036|lt=Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine|1=Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine (Hyōgo)}} in {{ill|Miki, Hyōgo|en|Miki, Hyōgo|qid=Q31238|lt=Miki City|1=Miki, Hyōgo}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|qid=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}]] '''{{ill|Hiraoka Shrine|en|Hiraoka Shrine|qid=Q32422|lt=Hiraka Shrine|1=Hiraoka Shrine}} Autumn Festival''' (October 14 and 15) * Izumoi/Torii: 1 large taiko, 1 small taiko * Nukata: 1 large taiko, 1 medium taiko, 1 small taiko * Treasure Box: 1 large taiko, 1 small taiko * Toyora: 1 large taiko, 1 small taiko * Kirikawa: 1 large taiko, 1 small taiko * Gojō: 1 large taiko, 1 small taiko * Kyakubō: 1 large taiko, 1 small taiko * Kawachi: 1 large taiko, 1 small taiko * Shijō: 1 large taiko, 4 small taiko, 1 float
'''{{ill|Daigase Shrine|ja|大賀世神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daigase Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135504235|1=Daigase Shrine}}''' (Second Saturday–Sunday of October) * Yokokoji
'''{{ill|Ikeshima Shrine|ja|池島神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikeshima Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135504237|1=Ikeshima Shrine}}''' (October 21–22) * Inui * Tatsumi * Honchō * Hashizume
'''{{ill|Kajinashi Shrine|ja|梶無神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kajinashi Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135504239|1=Kajinashi Shrine}}''' (October 22–23) * Sakurai * Kami Rokumonji * Shimorokumonji
'''{{ill|Yoshida Kasuga Shrine|ja|吉田春日神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshida Kasuga Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135504243|1=Yoshida Kasuga Shrine}}''' (October 15–16) * Hongō * Ichiba * Shinka * Gen * Shimoshima
'''{{ill|Ishikirizuken Shrine|ja|石切劔箭神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishikirizuken Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 石切剣箭神社|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 石切剣箭神社|1=Ishikiri Tsurugiya Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11585189}}''' (Summer: July 2–4; Autumn: Third Saturday–Sunday of October) * Uetsuke * Tsujiko * Shiba * Kusah
*Note: Taiko platforms participating in the summer do not take part in the autumn festival.
'''{{ill|Ōtsu Shrine|ja|大津神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtsu Shrine|qq=}}''' (Second Saturday–Sunday of October) * Furumizuasobi * Machimizuasobi
'''{{ill|Mitsurugi Shrine Summer Grand Festival|ja|御劔神社 夏季大祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mitsurugi Shrine Summer Grand Festival|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=DELETED_QID|1=Q135504246}}''' (Third Saturday–Sunday of July) * Honbu: 1 mikoshi, 1 small mikoshi, 1 small taiko * Kitano-machi: 1 large taiko * Nishino-machi: 1 large taiko * Shinmachi: 1 large taiko * Chūnanomachi: 1 large taiko, 1 small taiko
== Futon Taiko in Yao City == '''{{ill|Tamaso Shrine (Yao City)|ja|玉祖神社 (八尾市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamaso Shrine (Yao City)|1=Tamanooya Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11572730}}''' (Saturday–Sunday immediately preceding {{ill|Marine Day|en|Marine Day|qid=Q1355833|lt=Marine Day|1=Marine Day}} in July) * Kandachi * Mizukoshi * Rakuneji * Ōtake * Chizuka * Hattori-gawa * Ōkubō * Korikawa * Yamabatake * Kurotani
'''{{ill|Manganji|ja|東山本町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manganji|1=Higashiyamamotochō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11526717}}''' (associated with {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine|en|Sumiyoshi Shrine|qid=Q32512|lt=Sumiyoshi Shrine|1=Sumiyoshi Shrine}}, [https://hachimanjinjya.masuda.org/], {{ill|Shikibu Onoyakunin Shrine|ja|式部御野縣主神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikibu Onoyakunin Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135504248|1=Shikibu Onoyakunin Shrine}}) (Last Saturday–Sunday of July) * Dōgaitachi (from Sumiyoshi Shrine) * [https://hachimanjinjya.masuda.org/] * Shikibu (from Shikibu Onoyakunin Shrine) * Shinka (from Sumiyoshi Shrine)
(Last day of July, Monday) {{ill|Yahagi Shrine|ja|矢作神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yahagi Shrine|1=Yahagi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11583920}}
'''{{ill|Onchi Shrine|ja|恩智神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onchi Shrine|1=Onji Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11492258}}''' (Summer Festival: August 1; Autumn Festival: November 26)
'''{{ill|Yasaka Shrine|ja|八阪神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yasaka Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 八坂神社 (曖昧さ回避)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 八坂神社 (曖昧さ回避)}}''' (Last Saturday–Sunday of July) * Nishiyamamoto (Shell Mound type)
'''{{ill|Kamitsuji Shrine (Yao City)|ja|神劔神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsuji Shrine (Yao City)|1=Mitsurugi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11588775}}''' (Last Saturday–Sunday of July)
'''{{ill|Yuge Shrine (Yao City)|ja|弓削神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yuge Shrine (Yao City)|1=Yuge Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11487075}}''' (Last Saturday–Sunday of July)
'''{{ill|Kajira Shrine|ja|加津良神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kajira Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135504250|1=Kajira Shrine}}''' (Second week of October, Saturday–Sunday)
{{ill|Onoyakunin Shrine|ja|御野縣主神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onoyakunin Shrine|1=Mino Agatanushi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11489340}} (Uenoshima) (Second Saturday–Sunday of October)
'''{{ill|Sanjūhachi Shrine|ja|三十八神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanjūhachi Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135504253|1=Sanjūhachi Shrine}}''' (Third week of October, Saturday–Sunday)
== Futon Taiko in Fujiidera City == '''{{ill|Karakuni Shrine|ja|辛国神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Karakuni Shrine|qq=}} • {{ill|Hachiman shrine|lt=Hachiman shrine|lt=Hachiman Shrine|ja|八幡神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 八幡宮|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 八幡宮}} • {{ill|Ubusuji Shrine|ja|産土神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ubusuji Shrine|1=Ubusuna Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11574976}}''' (Saturday–Sunday immediately preceding {{ill|Sports Day|ja|体育の日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sports Day|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スポーツの日 (日本)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スポーツの日 (日本)|1=Sports Day (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1359602}} in October) * Nakaoka (at {{ill|Karakuni Shrine|ja|辛国神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Karakuni Shrine|qq=}}) * Tsudō (at {{ill|Hachiman shrine|lt=Hachiman shrine|lt=Hachiman Shrine|ja|八幡神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 八幡宮|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 八幡宮}}) * Koyama (Sakai-type) (at {{ill|Ubusuji Shrine|ja|産土神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ubusuji Shrine|1=Ubusuna Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11574976}}) * Kitaoka
== Futon Taiko in Osaka City == '''{{ill|Ikuno-ku, Osaka|en|Ikuno-ku, Osaka|qid=Q490520|lt=Ikuno Ward|1=Ikuno-ku, Osaka}}''' * {{ill|Tatsumi Shrine|ja|巽神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatsumi Shrine|1=Tatsumi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11479955}} (Summer: July 14–15; Autumn: October 14–15) * Daichi '''{{ill|Suminoe-ku, Osaka|en|Suminoe-ku, Osaka|qid=Q985711|lt=Suminoe Ward|1=Suminoe-ku, Osaka}}''' * {{ill|Takasaki Shrine|ja|高崎神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takasaki Shrine|1=Takasaki Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11669785}} (Saturday–Sunday around mid-July) * Minamikagaya '''{{ill|Hirano-ku, Osaka|en|Hirano-ku, Osaka|qid=Q861256|lt=Hirano Ward|1=Hirano-ku, Osaka}}''' * {{ill|Kuaisen Shrine|ja|杭全神社|1=Kumata Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuaisen Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumata Shrine|qq=}} (July 11–14) : Each year a town is assigned on a rotating basis (once every nine years) to organize the parade; futon taiko are carried only on the 11th and 14th. In this area the child carriers—called “{{ill|tatakiko|ja|叩き児|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tatakiko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}” (taiko strikers)—are typically boys aged about 3 to 12 who wear heavy ceremonial makeup, lavish costumes, and headscarves draped with square futon. * {{ill|Nagayoshishiki Shrine|ja|長吉志紀神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagayoshishiki Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135504259|1=Nagayoshishiki Shrine}} (Second Saturday–Sunday of October) '''{{ill|Nishiyodogawa-ku, Osaka|en|Nishiyodogawa-ku, Osaka|qid=Q985765|lt=Nishiyodogawa Ward|1=Nishiyodogawa-ku, Osaka}}''' * {{ill|Taminos Shrine|ja|田蓑神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taminos Shrine|1=Tamino Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11577264}} (July 31–August 1) '''{{ill|Konohana-ku, Osaka|en|Konohana-ku, Osaka|qid=Q960258|lt=Konohana Ward|1=Konohana-ku, Osaka}}''' * {{ill|Miohyō Sumiyoshi Shrine|ja|澪標住吉神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miohyō Sumiyoshi Shrine|1=Miotsukushi Sumiyoshi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11566022}} (July 31–August 1) * {{ill|Karasuya|ja|鴉宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Karasuya|1=Karasuya|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11675499}} (July 31–August 1) * {{ill|Sando Shrine|ja|産土神社 (大阪市此花区)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sando Shrine|1=Ubusuna Shrine (Konohana, Osaka)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11574978}} (July 22–23)
== Futon Taiko in Sakai City == [[File:Osaka Sakai Nishiminato Futon Taiko.jpg|thumbnail|The futon taiko in Sakai City features futon-like elements attached around it, forming a structure with a hollow interior for riders.]] [[File:AguchiHassaku 4narabi.jpg|thumb|On September 8, Reiwa 6, at {{ill|Kaikō Shrine|ja|開口神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaikō Shrine|1=Aguchi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11655187}} Hassaku Festival, the newly refurbished 5th-generation taiko platform of the {{ill|Shinzaike-hama|ja|新在家濱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinzaike-hama|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} was displayed in front of the main shrine for the first time in 10 years. From left to right: Ashiharahama, Shinzaike-hama, Daikōhama, Dainantogawa.]] [[File:Kaisenhama taiko.png|thumb|{{ill|Kaishenhama Taiko Platform|ja|海船濱太鼓台|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaishenhama Taiko Platform|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, made in {{ill|1909|en|1909|qid=Q2057|lt=Meiji 42|1=1909}}.]] [[File:Kitatogawa 2daimetaikodai.png|thumb|2nd-generation {{ill|Kitatogawa Taiko Platform|ja|北戸川太鼓台|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitatogawa Taiko Platform|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, newly refurbished in {{ill|2012|en|2012|qid=Q1990|lt=Heisei 24|1=2012}}.]][[File:Enoki 3daimetaiko.jpg|thumb|3rd-generation {{ill|Enoki Taiko Platform|ja|榎太鼓台|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enoki Taiko Platform|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, newly refurbished in {{ill|2006|en|2006|qid=Q2021|lt=Heisei 18|1=2006}}.]] '''{{ill|Kaikō Shrine|ja|開口神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaikō Shrine|1=Aguchi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11655187}} Hassaku Festival''' (on the Friday preceding the first Golden Day, Saturday, and Sunday) In {{ill|泉州 (disambiguation)|en|泉州 (disambiguation)|qid=Q11143129|lt=Senshū|1=泉州 (disambiguation)}}, the original Hassaku Festival featured futon taiko even before their formal dedication, with a history of about 600 years. At its peak, as many as 13 taiko platforms were paraded. * Ashiharahama * Dainantogawa/Sumida (Sakai-type) * Shinzaike-hama * Daikōhama (Note: In the 10th anniversary refurbishment, Daikōhama was changed to a futon taiko with red and white futon.) * Additionally, child taiko from Minamihanmachi also participate. * Daikōhama was revived after a hiatus of about 70 years by local volunteers. * From September 8, 2024, the Dainantogawa Sumida Youth Association was renamed the Dainantogawa Sumida Taiko Association. '''{{ill|Sugawara Shrine (Sakai)|ja|菅原神社 (堺市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara Shrine (Sakai)|1=Sugawara Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11619211}} Hassaku Festival''' (on the Friday preceding the first Golden Day, Saturday, and Sunday) * {{ill|Kaishenhama|ja|海船濱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaishenhama|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Sakai-type) * {{ill|Kitatogawa|ja|北戸川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitatogawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Sakai-type) Child taiko from Kitatogawa also participate. * Until 2017 ({{ill|2017|en|2017|qid=Q25290|lt=Heisei 29|1=2017}}), taiko platforms were typically paraded on September 13–15; however, due to lower participation and spectator numbers on weekdays compared to weekends, from 2018 ({{ill|2018|en|2018|qid=Q25291|lt=Heisei 30|1=2018}}) the schedule was changed to match that of the Kaikō Shrine Hassaku Festival (Friday preceding Golden Day, Saturday, and Sunday). * Before World War II, as many as 14 taiko platforms were paraded during the peak; subsequently, due to air raids and a shortage of carriers, many were abolished, and currently only 2 remain in the parade. * In addition to futon taiko, Kitatogawa also features a lion dance called “{{ill|Togawa no Shishi|ja|戸川之獅子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Togawa no Shishi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}” which, aside from the Hassaku Festival, also appears at local Bon festivals and in events held in parks and schools. '''{{ill|Futondaiko Matsuri (Osaka)|ja|ふとん太鼓台祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futondaiko Matsuri (Osaka)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''' (Third Friday, Saturday, and Sunday of September) * Ishizutsukawamukai Alliance (comprising groups from Ishizutsukawamukai, Ishizuhigashi, Ishizuwakanaka, Kitawakanaka, Jūsanchō-kai, and Nishidōzō-kai) * Enoki * Dainantogawa Sumida Youth Association * Kitatogawa * Shinzaike-hama * Daikōhama * (Ishizutsukawamukai participates once every three years selected from among six towns) * Additionally, besides the taiko platforms, child taiko from Nankōmachi used to participate; however, they were once donated to Sakai City and later given to Calabasas, USA. '''{{ill|Hamada Shrine|ja|大賀世神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hamada Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135504235|1=Daigase Shrine}} Autumn Festival''' (Friday, Saturday, and Sunday of the first week of October) * Ishizutsukawamukai Alliance (Ishizutsukawamukai, Ishizuhigashi, Ishizuwakanaka, Kitawakanaka, Jūsanchō-kai, Nishidōzō-kai) * Enoki * Dainantogawa Sumida Youth Association * Nanantogawa * (Note: Prior to World War II, three taiko platforms were paraded; due to air raids many were destroyed, and currently only the Enoki remains.) '''{{ill|Hyōda Shrine|ja|大津神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōda Shrine|1=Ōtsu Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11437359}}''' Autumn Festival (Friday, Saturday, and Sunday of the first week of October) *[http://hamaishi.web.fc2.com/] *[http://www.rakukokai.com/] *[http://taikodai.web.fc2.com/] *[https://futondaiko.jugem.jp/] *[http://hamaishi.web.fc2.com/] *[http://hiwasahachiman.com/] *[http://www2.ocn.ne.jp/~nishi000/danjiri/danuta/] == (The article continues with sections on futon taiko in other regions – such as in Sakai City, Kaizuka City, Izumisano City, Ibaraki City, Nara City, and elsewhere – as well as historical notes, chants and shouts, details on preserved and abolished taiko platforms, and further regional variations.) == == History == The futon taiko of the former {{ill|Sakai|en|Sakai|qid=Q193428|lt=Sakai City|1=Sakai}} are said to have been carried as early as the mid-Edo period during the festivals of {{ill|Sumiyoshi-taisha|en|Sumiyoshi-taisha|qid=Q705949|lt=Sumiyoshi Taisha|1=Sumiyoshi-taisha}}, as depicted on the “{{nihongo|Mimura Miyamatsuri Ema|ja|三村宮祭礼絵馬|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mimura Miyamatsuri Ema|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.” In Sakai City, until roughly the mid-Meiji period, during festivals each parishioner group would pull floats and halberds, and taiko platforms existed as part of the ritual. However, on a festival held on August 1 (old calendar) in {{ill|1896|en|1896|qid=Q7846|lt=Meiji 29|1=1896}}, on a narrow section of the {{ill|Kishu Highway|ja|紀州街道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kishu Highway|1=Kishū Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11605050}} in Nakanomachi West (narrow enough for only one float), a collision occurred between a boat float from the Minato group and a float from the northern blacksmith district, resulting in a dispute and casualties. This incident, known as the “Sakai Float Incident,” led to a complete ban on parading elaborately decorated floats in the (old) Sakai area thereafter. Later, to celebrate Japan’s victory in the {{ill|Russo-Japanese War|en|Russo-Japanese War|qid=Q159950|lt=Russo-Japanese War|1=Russo-Japanese War}}, the parading of elaborately decorated floats was permitted again; however, because parishioners disposed of or sold their floats, many float craftsmen left Sakai or changed trades, and because of safety concerns and the fact that many regions began purchasing futon taiko from {{ill|Awaji|en|Awaji|qid=Q349322|lt=Awaji|1=Awaji}}, only the futon taiko remained. In the {{ill|Taishō period|ja|大正時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishō period|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大正|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大正|1=Taishō era|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q329174}}, at {{ill|Kaikō Shrine|ja|開口神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaikō Shrine|1=Aguchi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11655187}} as many as 13 taiko platforms and at {{ill|Sugawara Shrine (Sakai)|ja|菅原神社 (堺市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara Shrine (Sakai)|1=Sugawara Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11619211}} as many as 14 were offered, marking the golden age of Sakai’s futon taiko. Each district showcased its individuality through unique decorative features—such as non-white tassels, embroidered futon coverings (with, for example, Shinzaike-hama still using blue), and variations in tying methods. In the {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|qid=Q327567|lt=Shōwa period|1=Shōwa era}} labor shortages and other circumstances led some parishioners to relinquish their taiko platforms; later, many futon taiko were destroyed in the {{ill|Sakai Air Raid|ja|堺大空襲|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakai Air Raid|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大阪大空襲|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大阪大空襲|1=Bombing of Osaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q2814395}}. In the postwar period, due to material shortages, decoration reverted to a basic style; however, in recent years there has been a revival of the lavish prewar style. Since 1974, futon taiko have participated in the {{ill|Sakai Matsuri|en|Sakai Matsuri|qid=Q11428095|lt=Sakai Festival|1=Sakai Matsuri}} and its eve festival. Initially only those from Ashiharahama, Dainantogawa, Kitatogawa, and Shinzaike-hama participated; later 10 districts joined. All these taiko platforms are members of the {{ill|Sakai Festival Futon Taiko Preservation Union|ja|堺まつりふとん太鼓連合保存会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakai Festival Futon Taiko Preservation Union|1=Sakai Festival Futon Taiko Preservation Union|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11428096}}. * Note: Ashiharahama, Dejima, Nishiminato, and Higashiminato withdrew in 2019. In 1991, Ashiharahama, in 1993 Dejima, and in 2002 the Ishizutsukawamukai (specifically Ishizuwakanaka) represented the union in the {{ill|Midosuji Parade|ja|御堂筋パレード|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Midosuji Parade|1=Midosuji Parade|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11488902}}. On May 1, 2019, a commemorative ceremony was held at {{ill|Daisen Park|ja|大仙公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisen Park|qq=}}, centered on the Sakai Festival Futon Taiko Preservation Union, to mark the accession of the new Emperor. A total of 11 taiko platforms (from Ishizutsukawamukai, Enoki, Dainantogawa, Enokicho, Kaishenhama, Kitatogawa, Shinzaike-hama, Daikōhama, Nishiminato, Hamaishi, and Higashiminato) participated. The ceremony also saw participation from districts (such as Enokicho, Kaishenhama, and Hamaishi) that do not normally take part in the Sakai Festival. Due to the impact of the {{ill|Novel coronavirus|en|Novel coronavirus|qid=Q84497971|lt=novel coronavirus|1=Novel coronavirus}}, in 2020 and 2021 the parading of taiko platforms was suspended in various regions. == Chants and Shouts == 【Carrying Call】 "Yoitoyaiyamaka, Dokkoisa-no-se (Sōryā – dekita! Dekita!)" 【Festival Song】 *Verse 1* "Autumn moon over {{ill|Ishiyama|ja|石山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishiyama|1=Ishiyama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q15890429}}, Clouds gather amid the flowers borne by the wind; The news carried by the wind comes from the island of {{ill|Awa|ja|阿波|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Awa|1=Q11657489|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11657489}}, And in a striped wallet, five or ten ryo; What is that rumbling sound? After earthquakes and thunder comes the evening shower— Berabera, berashoshoshoi!" *Verse 2* "Peonies with Chinese lion motifs, Bamboo where tigers chase— In pursuit of the noble one, lending wisdom; At {{ill|Chūzan Seikan-ji|ja|中山清閑寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūzan Seikan-ji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135490861|1=Seikan-ji Temple}}, The resident of Seikan-ji is a monk, Thus it is the Hassaku rain— Berabera, berashoshoshoi!" *(In some districts the line “Thus it is the Hassaku rain” is omitted.)* *Verse 3* *(Once sung mainly in the old city district; currently preserved only in select areas such as Kaishenhama.)* In addition, many districts have their own original festival songs. 【Victory Shout】 "Yāe, Yāe, Āyoiyamakasokojaina, Yōiyase!" == Preserved Taiko Platforms == In {{ill|Sakai-ku, Sakai City|ja|堺市堺区|1=Sakai-ku, Sakai|2=ja|3=堺区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakai-ku, Sakai City|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1042008}} – at the {{ill|Sakai City Museum|en|Sakai City Museum|qid=Q1448918|lt=Sakai City Museum|1=Sakai City Museum}} * Honchō (Predecessor generation) In {{ill|Sakai-ku, Sakai City|ja|堺市堺区|1=Sakai-ku, Sakai|2=ja|3=堺区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakai-ku, Sakai City|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1042008}}, within the warehouse on the grounds of {{ill|Gekkō Shrine|ja|月洲神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gekkō Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135490862|1=Tsukisu Shrine}} in {{ill|Minamishimamachi|ja|南嶋町|comment=I know this is not a translation but it is actually true and I think this might be not a real location|1=Sakai|2=ja|3=堺市|qid=Q193428|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamishimamachi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * Namimachi (abolished) Note: Not open to the public. In Kita-Tabori-chō, Sakai City – inside the compound of {{ill|Sugawara Shrine|ja|菅原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara Shrine|qq=}} at Nan-dōgahama * Kai (Kitakai) (suspended) Note: Not open to the public. == Abolished Futon Taiko == Due to air raids during the {{ill|World War II|en|World War II|qid=Q362|lt=Second World War|1=World War II}} and postwar carrier shortages, many taiko platforms were abolished. At the peak in the old city district there were roughly 30 district groups’ worth of futon taiko—comparable to the total number now in Sakai City. (Old City District) {{ill|Sugawara Shrine|ja|菅原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara Shrine|qq=}} * Kitajima * Kitahama * Kunigahama * Shakurimono * Yadohama * Ueda * Namimatsu * Kai/Kitakai * Minatogawa * (Others) {{ill|Kaikō Shrine|ja|開口神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaikō Shrine|1=Aguchi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11655187}} * Ōhama * Kenchū-dai (or Daikumi-dai) * Konya-hama * (Various groups) * Daikōhama (revived in {{ill|1998|en|1998|qid=Q2089|lt=Heisei 10|1=1998}}) * Nakadohama * Nakategawa * Higashidaikō * (Others) {{ill|Katagae Shrine|ja|方違神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katagae Shrine|1=Katamigae Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11504380}} * Kawaramachi * Hanadaguchi {{ill|Gekkō Shrine|ja|月洲神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gekkō Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135490862|1=Tsukisu Shrine}} * Minashima {{ill|Tamorikami Shrine|ja|田守神社|1=Tamorino Shrine|2=ja|3=田守神社|qid=Q135039249|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamorikami Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * Matsuya {{ill|Nonomiya Shrine|en|Nonomiya Shrine|qid=Q501266|lt=Nonomiya Shrine|1=Nonomiya Shrine}} * Nakanoshinden (later transferred to floats; now in Higashimachi) * Hatayama (transferred to floats; now in Hatayama-chō) == Taiko Platforms in Kaizuka City == '''''{{ill|Kanda Shrine|ja|感田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda Shrine|qq=}} Summer Festival (Adult Taiko Platforms) • Autumn Festival (Child Taiko)''''' [[File:Night in Kaizuka Taiko Platform.jpg|thumb|A Kaizuka taiko platform decorated with lanterns. In recent years, the South Taiko has also adopted a light-up method from {{ill|2017|en|2017|qid=Q25290|lt=Heisei 29|1=2017}}; however, from a traditional standpoint some oppose imitating other regions. In the second year, the evening uses “lanterns” while the main shrine uses “light-up.”|alt=|left|283x283px]] [[File:0minaminisi.jpg|thumb|In {{ill|Taishō 8|ja|大正八年|1=1919|2=ja|3=1919年|lt=Taishō 8|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishō 8|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q2157}} at the main shrine in {{ill|Kaizuka Hama|ja|貝塚浜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaizuka Hama|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a newly refurbished West Taiko Platform is shown (note that the belt is still white at first and turns black over subsequent years). This is a masterpiece by {{ill|Yoshikuni Sakurai|ja|桜井義国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikuni Sakurai|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=DELETED_QID|1=Q135493131}}, executed with full vigor. The left platform is the lost predecessor South Taiko (said to have been burned in a warehouse fire due to a plague outbreak).|alt=|left|286x286px]] The Hassaku Festival at {{ill|Kaizuka Shrine|ja|感田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaizuka Shrine|1=Kanda Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11494969}} (officially “{{ill|Kaizuka Miya Taiko Platform Festival|ja|貝塚宮太鼓台祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaizuka Miya Taiko Platform Festival|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}”) is held on the Friday immediately preceding Golden Day, Saturday, and Sunday. In {{ill|泉州 (disambiguation)|en|泉州 (disambiguation)|qid=Q11143129|lt=Senshū|1=泉州 (disambiguation)}}, the original Hassaku Festival featured futon taiko long before their formal dedication, with a history of about 600 years. At its peak, 13 taiko platforms were paraded. * Ashiharahama * Dainantogawa/Sumida (Sakai-type) * Shinzaike-hama * Daikōhama (Note: At one time, Daikōhama was converted to a futon taiko with red and white futon during its 10th-anniversary refurbishment.) * Additionally, child taiko from Minamihanmachi participate. * Daikōhama was revived after roughly 70 years by local volunteers. * From September 8, 2024, the Dainantogawa Sumida Youth Association was renamed the Dainantogawa Sumida Taiko Association. '''{{ill|Sugawara Shrine (Sakai)|ja|菅原神社 (堺市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara Shrine (Sakai)|1=Sugawara Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11619211}} Hassaku Festival''' (held on the Friday immediately preceding Golden Day, Saturday, and Sunday) * {{ill|Kaishenhama|ja|海船濱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaishenhama|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Sakai-type) * {{ill|Kitatogawa|ja|北戸川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitatogawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Sakai-type) Child taiko from Kitatogawa also participate. * Until 2017 ({{ill|2017|en|2017|qid=Q25290|lt=Heisei 29|1=2017}}) taiko platforms were typically paraded on September 13–15; however, due to lower participation on weekdays compared to holidays, from 2018 ({{ill|2018|en|2018|qid=Q25291|lt=Heisei 30|1=2018}}) the schedule was changed to match that of the Kaikō Shrine Hassaku Festival (Friday preceding Golden Day, Saturday, and Sunday). * Before World War II, as many as 14 taiko platforms were paraded at the peak; later, many were abolished due to air raids and a shortage of carriers, and currently only 2 are paraded. * In addition to futon taiko, Kitatogawa also features a lion dance known as “{{ill|Togawa no Shishi|ja|戸川之獅子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Togawa no Shishi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}” which, apart from the Hassaku Festival, also appears at local Bon festivals and at park and school events. == (Further sections follow for taiko platforms in other cities such as Izumisano, Ibaraki, Nara, and additional regions; as well as historical notes, chants and calls, details on preserved and abolished platforms, and related external links.) == == History == The futon taiko of the former {{ill|Sakai|en|Sakai|qid=Q193428|lt=Sakai City|1=Sakai}} are said to have been carried as early as the mid-Edo period during the festivals of {{ill|Sumiyoshi-taisha|en|Sumiyoshi-taisha|qid=Q705949|lt=Sumiyoshi Taisha|1=Sumiyoshi-taisha}}, as depicted on the “{{nihongo|Mimura Miyamatsuri Ema|ja|三村宮祭礼絵馬|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mimura Miyamatsuri Ema|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.” In Sakai City, until about the mid-Meiji period, during festivals each local parishioner group would pull floats and halberds, and taiko platforms were part of the ritual. However, on a festival held on August 1 (old calendar) in {{ill|1896|en|1896|qid=Q7846|lt=Meiji 29|1=1896}}, on a narrow section of the {{ill|Kishu Highway|ja|紀州街道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kishu Highway|1=Kishū Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11605050}} in Nakanomachi West (narrow enough for only one float), a collision occurred between a boat float from the Minato group and a float from the northern blacksmith district, resulting in a dispute and casualties. This incident, known as the “Sakai Float Incident,” led to a complete ban on parading elaborately decorated floats in the old Sakai area thereafter. Later, to celebrate Japan’s victory in the {{ill|Russo-Japanese War|en|Russo-Japanese War|qid=Q159950|lt=Russo-Japanese War|1=Russo-Japanese War}}, the parading of elaborately decorated floats was permitted again; however, since parishioners disposed of or sold their floats, many float craftsmen left Sakai or changed trades, and concerns over safety—plus many regions purchasing futon taiko from {{ill|Awaji|en|Awaji|qid=Q349322|lt=Awaji|1=Awaji}}—led to only the futon taiko remaining. In the {{ill|Taishō period|ja|大正時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishō period|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大正|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大正|1=Taishō era|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q329174}}, at {{ill|Kaikō Shrine|ja|開口神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaikō Shrine|1=Aguchi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11655187}} as many as 13 taiko platforms and at {{ill|Sugawara Shrine (Sakai)|ja|菅原神社 (堺市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara Shrine (Sakai)|1=Sugawara Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11619211}} as many as 14 were offered, marking the golden age of Sakai’s futon taiko. Each district expressed its individuality through unique decorative features—such as tassels in colors other than white, embroidered futon coverings (for example, Shinzaike-hama still uses blue), and varied tying methods. In the {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|qid=Q327567|lt=Shōwa period|1=Shōwa era}}, due to labor shortages and other circumstances, some parishioners began to relinquish their taiko platforms, and later many futon taiko were lost in the {{ill|Sakai Air Raid|ja|堺大空襲|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakai Air Raid|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大阪大空襲|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大阪大空襲|1=Bombing of Osaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q2814395}}. In the postwar period, due to shortages of materials, decorations reverted to a basic style; however, in recent years there has been a revival toward the lavish prewar style. Since 1974, futon taiko have participated in the {{ill|Sakai Matsuri|en|Sakai Matsuri|qid=Q11428095|lt=Sakai Festival|1=Sakai Matsuri}} and its eve festival. Initially, only taiko from Ashiharahama, Dainantogawa, Kitatogawa, and Shinzaike-hama participated; later, 10 district groups joined. All these taiko platforms are members of the {{ill|Sakai Festival Futon Taiko Preservation Union|ja|堺まつりふとん太鼓連合保存会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakai Festival Futon Taiko Preservation Union|1=Sakai Festival Futon Taiko Preservation Union|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11428096}}. * Note: Ashiharahama, Dejima, Nishiminato, and Higashiminato withdrew in 2019. In 1991, Ashiharahama, in 1993 Dejima, and in 2002 the Ishizutsukawamukai (specifically Ishizuwakanaka) represented the union in the {{ill|Midosuji Parade|ja|御堂筋パレード|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Midosuji Parade|1=Midosuji Parade|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11488902}}. On May 1, 2019, a commemorative ceremony was held at {{ill|Daisen Park|ja|大仙公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisen Park|qq=}}, centered on the Sakai Festival Futon Taiko Preservation Union, to mark the accession of the new Emperor. Eleven taiko platforms (from Ishizutsukawamukai, Enoki, Dainantogawa, Enokicho, Kaishenhama, Kitatogawa, Shinzaike-hama, Daikōhama, Nishiminato, Hamaishi, and Higashiminato) participated. The ceremony also saw participation from districts (such as Enokicho, Kaishenhama, and Hamaishi) that do not normally participate in the Sakai Festival. Due to the impact of the {{ill|Novel coronavirus|en|Novel coronavirus|qid=Q84497971|lt=novel coronavirus|1=Novel coronavirus}}, in 2020 and 2021 the parading of taiko platforms was suspended in various regions. == Chants and Calls == 【Carrying Call】 "Yoitoyaiyamaka, Dokkoisa-no-se (Sōryā – dekita! Dekita!)" 【Festival Song】 *Verse 1* "Autumn moon over {{ill|Ishiyama|ja|石山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishiyama|1=Ishiyama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q15890429}}, Clouds gather amidst clustered blossoms borne on the wind; News rides the breeze from the island of {{ill|Awa|ja|阿波|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Awa|1=Q11657489|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11657489}}, And in a striped wallet, five or ten ryo; What is that rumbling sound? After earthquakes and thunder comes the evening shower— Berabera, berashoshoshoi!" *Verse 2* "Peonies with Chinese lion motifs, Bamboo pursued by tigers— Chasing the noble one, lending wisdom; At {{ill|Chūzan Seikan-ji|ja|中山清閑寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūzan Seikan-ji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135490861|1=Seikan-ji Temple}}, The resident is a monk, Thus it is the Hassaku rain— Berabera, berashoshoshoi!" *(In some renditions the line “Thus it is the Hassaku rain” is omitted.)* *Verse 3* *(Once sung mainly in the old city district; now preserved only in select areas such as Kaishenhama.)* In addition, many districts have their own original festival songs. 【Victory Shout】 "Yāe, Yāe, Āyoiyamakasokojaina, Yōiyase!" == Preserved Taiko Platforms == In {{ill|Sakai-ku, Sakai City|ja|堺市堺区|1=Sakai-ku, Sakai|2=ja|3=堺区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakai-ku, Sakai City|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1042008}} – at the {{ill|Sakai City Museum|en|Sakai City Museum|qid=Q1448918|lt=Sakai City Museum|1=Sakai City Museum}} * Honchō (Predecessor generation) In {{ill|Sakai-ku, Sakai City|ja|堺市堺区|1=Sakai-ku, Sakai|2=ja|3=堺区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakai-ku, Sakai City|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1042008}}, within the warehouse on the grounds of {{ill|Gekkō Shrine|ja|月洲神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gekkō Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135490862|1=Tsukisu Shrine}} in {{ill|Minamishimamachi|ja|南嶋町|comment=I know this is not a translation but it is actually true and I think this might be not a real location|1=Sakai|2=ja|3=堺市|qid=Q193428|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamishimamachi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * Namimachi (abolished) Note: Not open to the public. In Kita-Tabori-chō, Sakai City – inside the compound of {{ill|Sugawara Shrine|ja|菅原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara Shrine|qq=}} at Nan-dōgahama * Kai (Kitakai) (suspended) Note: Not open to the public. == Abolished Futon Taiko == Due to air raids during the {{ill|World War II|en|World War II|qid=Q362|lt=Second World War|1=World War II}} and subsequent carrier shortages, many taiko platforms were abolished. At the peak in the old city district, there were roughly 30 district groups’ worth of futon taiko – comparable to the total number currently existing in Sakai City. (Old City District) {{ill|Sugawara Shrine|ja|菅原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara Shrine|qq=}} * Kitajima * Kitahama * Kunigahama * Shakurimono * Yadohama * Ueda * Namimatsu * Kai/Kitakai * Minatogawa * (Others) {{ill|Kaikō Shrine|ja|開口神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaikō Shrine|1=Aguchi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11655187}} * Ōhama * Kenchū-dai (or Daikumi-dai) * Konya-hama * (Various groups) * Daikōhama (revived in {{ill|1998|en|1998|qid=Q2089|lt=Heisei 10|1=1998}}) * Nakadohama * Nakategawa * Higashidaikō * (Others) {{ill|Katagae Shrine|ja|方違神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katagae Shrine|1=Katamigae Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11504380}} * Kawaramachi * Hanadaguchi {{ill|Gekkō Shrine|ja|月洲神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gekkō Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135490862|1=Tsukisu Shrine}} * Minashima {{ill|Tamorikami Shrine|ja|田守神社|1=Tamorino Shrine|2=ja|3=田守神社|qid=Q135039249|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamorikami Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * Matsuya {{ill|Nonomiya Shrine|en|Nonomiya Shrine|qid=Q501266|lt=Nonomiya Shrine|1=Nonomiya Shrine}} * Nakanoshinden (later transferred to floats; now in Higashimachi) * Hatayama (transferred to floats; now in Hatayama-chō) == Taiko Platforms in Kaizuka City == '''''{{ill|Kanda Shrine|ja|感田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda Shrine|qq=}} Summer Festival (Adult Taiko Platforms) • Autumn Festival (Child Taiko)''''' [[File:Night in Kaizuka Taiko Platform.jpg|thumb|A Kaizuka taiko platform decorated with lanterns. In recent years, the South Taiko has also adopted a light-up method from {{ill|2017|en|2017|qid=Q25290|lt=Heisei 29|1=2017}}; however, from a traditional viewpoint some oppose imitating other regions. In the second year, the evening uses “lanterns” while the main shrine uses “light-up.”|alt=|left|283x283px]] [[File:0minaminisi.jpg|thumb|In {{ill|Taishō 8|ja|大正八年|1=1919|2=ja|3=1919年|lt=Taishō 8|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishō 8|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q2157}} at the main shrine of {{ill|Kaizuka Hama|ja|貝塚浜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaizuka Hama|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a newly refurbished West Taiko Platform is shown (note that the belt is initially white and turns black over subsequent years). This is a masterpiece by {{ill|Yoshikuni Sakurai|ja|桜井義国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikuni Sakurai|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=DELETED_QID|1=Q135493131}}, executed with full vigor. The left platform is the lost predecessor South Taiko (said to have been burned in a warehouse fire due to a plague outbreak).|alt=|left|286x286px]] The Hassaku Festival at {{ill|Kaizuka Shrine|ja|感田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaizuka Shrine|1=Kanda Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11494969}} (officially “{{ill|Kaizuka Miya Taiko Platform Festival|ja|貝塚宮太鼓台祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaizuka Miya Taiko Platform Festival|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}”) is held on the Friday immediately preceding Golden Day, Saturday, and Sunday. In {{ill|泉州 (disambiguation)|en|泉州 (disambiguation)|qid=Q11143129|lt=Senshū|1=泉州 (disambiguation)}}, the original Hassaku Festival featured futon taiko even before their formal dedication, with a history of about 600 years. At its peak, 13 taiko platforms were paraded. * Ashiharahama * Dainantogawa/Sumida (Sakai-type) * Shinzaike-hama * Daikōhama (Note: At one point, Daikōhama was converted to a futon taiko with red and white futon during its 10th-anniversary refurbishment.) * Additionally, child taiko from Minamihanmachi participate. * Daikōhama was revived after roughly 70 years by local volunteers. * From September 8, 2024, the Dainantogawa Sumida Youth Association has been renamed the Dainantogawa Sumida Taiko Association. '''{{ill|Sugawara Shrine (Sakai)|ja|菅原神社 (堺市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara Shrine (Sakai)|1=Sugawara Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11619211}} Hassaku Festival''' (held on the Friday immediately preceding Golden Day, Saturday, and Sunday) * {{ill|Kaishenhama|ja|海船濱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaishenhama|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Sakai-type) * {{ill|Kitatogawa|ja|北戸川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitatogawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Sakai-type) Child taiko from Kitatogawa also participate. * Until 2017 ({{ill|2017|en|2017|qid=Q25290|lt=Heisei 29|1=2017}}) taiko platforms were typically paraded on September 13–15; however, due to lower turnout on weekdays compared to holidays, from 2018 ({{ill|2018|en|2018|qid=Q25291|lt=Heisei 30|1=2018}}) the schedule was changed to match that of the Kaikō Shrine Hassaku Festival (Friday preceding Golden Day, Saturday, and Sunday). * Before World War II, as many as 14 taiko platforms were paraded at the peak; subsequently, many were abolished due to air raids and carrier shortages, and currently only 2 are paraded. * In addition to futon taiko, Kitatogawa also features a lion dance known as “{{ill|Togawa no Shishi|ja|戸川之獅子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Togawa no Shishi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}” which, apart from the Hassaku Festival, also appears at local Bon festivals and at events in parks and schools. == (Additional sections follow for taiko platforms in other cities such as Izumisano, Ibaraki, Nara, and further regions; as well as sections on history, chants, preserved and abolished platforms, and related external links.) == == History == The futon taiko of the former {{ill|Sakai|en|Sakai|qid=Q193428|lt=Sakai City|1=Sakai}} are said to have been carried as early as the mid-Edo period during the festivals of {{ill|Sumiyoshi-taisha|en|Sumiyoshi-taisha|qid=Q705949|lt=Sumiyoshi Taisha|1=Sumiyoshi-taisha}}, as depicted on the “{{nihongo|Mimura Miyamatsuri Ema|ja|三村宮祭礼絵馬|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mimura Miyamatsuri Ema|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.” In Sakai City, until roughly the mid-Meiji period, during festivals each local parishioner group would pull floats and halberds, and taiko platforms were integral to the ritual. However, on a festival held on August 1 (old calendar) in {{ill|1896|en|1896|qid=Q7846|lt=Meiji 29|1=1896}}, on a narrow section of the {{ill|Kishu Highway|ja|紀州街道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kishu Highway|1=Kishū Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11605050}} in Nakanomachi West (narrow enough for only one float), a collision occurred between a boat float from the Minato group and a float from the northern blacksmith district, resulting in a dispute and casualties. This incident, known as the “Sakai Float Incident,” led to a complete ban on parading elaborately decorated floats in the old Sakai area thereafter. Later, to celebrate Japan’s victory in the {{ill|Russo-Japanese War|en|Russo-Japanese War|qid=Q159950|lt=Russo-Japanese War|1=Russo-Japanese War}}, the parading of elaborately decorated floats was permitted again; however, because parishioners disposed of or sold their floats, many float craftsmen left Sakai or changed trades, and concerns over safety—and the fact that many regions began purchasing futon taiko from {{ill|Awaji|en|Awaji|qid=Q349322|lt=Awaji|1=Awaji}}—resulted in only futon taiko remaining. In the {{ill|Taishō period|ja|大正時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishō period|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大正|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大正|1=Taishō era|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q329174}}, at {{ill|Kaikō Shrine|ja|開口神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaikō Shrine|1=Aguchi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11655187}} as many as 13 taiko platforms and at {{ill|Sugawara Shrine (Sakai)|ja|菅原神社 (堺市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara Shrine (Sakai)|1=Sugawara Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11619211}} as many as 14 were offered, marking the golden age of Sakai’s futon taiko. Each district expressed its individuality through unique decorative features—such as tassels in colors other than white, embroidered futon coverings (for instance, Shinzaike-hama still uses blue), and varied tying methods. In the {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|qid=Q327567|lt=Shōwa period|1=Shōwa era}}, due to labor shortages and other factors, some parishioners began to relinquish their taiko platforms, and later many futon taiko were lost in the {{ill|Sakai Air Raid|ja|堺大空襲|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakai Air Raid|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大阪大空襲|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大阪大空襲|1=Bombing of Osaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q2814395}}. In the postwar period, because of material shortages, decorations reverted to a basic style; however, in recent years there has been a revival toward the lavish prewar style. Since 1974, futon taiko have participated in the {{ill|Sakai Matsuri|en|Sakai Matsuri|qid=Q11428095|lt=Sakai Festival|1=Sakai Matsuri}} and its eve festival. Initially, only those from Ashiharahama, Dainantogawa, Kitatogawa, and Shinzaike-hama participated; later, 10 district groups joined. All these taiko platforms are members of the {{ill|Sakai Festival Futon Taiko Preservation Union|ja|堺まつりふとん太鼓連合保存会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakai Festival Futon Taiko Preservation Union|1=Sakai Festival Futon Taiko Preservation Union|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11428096}}. * Note: Ashiharahama, Dejima, Nishiminato, and Higashiminato withdrew in 2019. In 1991, Ashiharahama; in 1993, Dejima; and in 2002, the Ishizutsukawamukai (specifically Ishizuwakanaka) represented the union in the {{ill|Midosuji Parade|ja|御堂筋パレード|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Midosuji Parade|1=Midosuji Parade|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11488902}}. On May 1, 2019, a commemorative ceremony was held at {{ill|Daisen Park|ja|大仙公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisen Park|qq=}}, centered on the Sakai Festival Futon Taiko Preservation Union, to mark the accession of the new Emperor. A total of 11 taiko platforms (from Ishizutsukawamukai, Enoki, Dainantogawa, Enokicho, Kaishenhama, Kitatogawa, Shinzaike-hama, Daikōhama, Nishiminato, Hamaishi, and Higashiminato) participated. The ceremony also saw participation from districts (such as Enokicho, Kaishenhama, and Hamaishi) that do not normally participate in the Sakai Festival. Due to the impact of the {{ill|Novel coronavirus|en|Novel coronavirus|qid=Q84497971|lt=novel coronavirus|1=Novel coronavirus}}, in 2020 and 2021 the parading of taiko platforms was suspended in various regions. == Chants and Shouts == 【Carrying Call】 "Yoitoyaiyamaka, Dokkoisa-no-se (Sōryā – dekita! In {{ill|泉州 (disambiguation)|en|泉州 (disambiguation)|qid=Q11143129|lt=Senshū|1=泉州 (disambiguation)}}, the original Hassaku Festival featured futon taiko even before their formal dedication, with a history of about 600 years. At its peak, 13 taiko platforms were paraded. * Ashiharahama * Dainantogawa/Sumida (Sakai-type) * Shinzaike-hama * Daikōhama (Note: At one time, Daikōhama was converted to a futon taiko with red and white futon during its 10th-anniversary refurbishment.) * Additionally, child taiko from Minamihanmachi participate. * Daikōhama was revived after roughly 70 years by local volunteers. * From September 8, 2024, the Dainantogawa Sumida Youth Association has been renamed the Dainantogawa Sumida Taiko Association. '''{{ill|Sugawara Shrine (Sakai)|ja|菅原神社 (堺市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara Shrine (Sakai)|1=Sugawara Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11619211}} Hassaku Festival''' (held on the Friday immediately preceding Golden Day, Saturday, and Sunday) * {{ill|Kaishenhama|ja|海船濱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaishenhama|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Sakai-type) * {{ill|Kitatogawa|ja|北戸川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitatogawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Sakai-type) Child taiko from Kitatogawa also participate. * Until 2017 ({{ill|2017|en|2017|qid=Q25290|lt=Heisei 29|1=2017}}) taiko platforms were typically paraded on September 13–15; however, due to lower turnout on weekdays compared to holidays, from 2018 ({{ill|2018|en|2018|qid=Q25291|lt=Heisei 30|1=2018}}) the schedule was changed to match that of the Kaikō Shrine Hassaku Festival (Friday preceding Golden Day, Saturday, and Sunday). * Before World War II, as many as 14 taiko platforms were paraded at the peak; subsequently, many were abolished due to air raids and a shortage of carriers, and currently only 2 are paraded. * In addition to futon taiko, Kitatogawa also features a lion dance called “{{ill|Togawa no Shishi|ja|戸川之獅子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Togawa no Shishi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}” which, aside from the Hassaku Festival, also appears at local Bon festivals and in park or school events. == (Additional sections follow for taiko platforms in other cities such as Izumisano, Ibaraki, Nara, and further regions; as well as sections on history, chants, preserved and abolished platforms, and related external links.) == == History == The futon taiko of the former {{ill|Sakai|en|Sakai|qid=Q193428|lt=Sakai City|1=Sakai}} are said to have been carried as early as the mid-Edo period during the festivals of {{ill|Sumiyoshi-taisha|en|Sumiyoshi-taisha|qid=Q705949|lt=Sumiyoshi Taisha|1=Sumiyoshi-taisha}}, as depicted on the “{{nihongo|Mimura Miyamatsuri Ema|ja|三村宮祭礼絵馬|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mimura Miyamatsuri Ema|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.” In Sakai City, until roughly the mid-Meiji period, each local parishioner group would pull floats and halberds during festivals, and taiko platforms were an integral part of the ritual. However, on a festival held on August 1 (old calendar) in {{ill|1896|en|1896|qid=Q7846|lt=Meiji 29|1=1896}}, on a narrow section of the {{ill|Kishu Highway|ja|紀州街道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kishu Highway|1=Kishū Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11605050}} in Nakanomachi West (narrow enough for only one float), a collision occurred between a boat float from the Minato group and a float from the northern blacksmith district, resulting in a dispute and casualties. This incident, known as the “Sakai Float Incident,” led to a complete ban on parading elaborately decorated floats in the old Sakai area thereafter. Later, to celebrate Japan’s victory in the {{ill|Russo-Japanese War|en|Russo-Japanese War|qid=Q159950|lt=Russo-Japanese War|1=Russo-Japanese War}}, the parading of elaborately decorated floats was permitted again; however, because parishioners disposed of or sold their floats, many float craftsmen left Sakai or changed trades, and concerns over safety—and the fact that many regions began purchasing futon taiko from {{ill|Awaji|en|Awaji|qid=Q349322|lt=Awaji|1=Awaji}}—resulted in only the futon taiko remaining. In the {{ill|Taishō period|ja|大正時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishō period|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大正|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大正|1=Taishō era|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q329174}}, at {{ill|Kaikō Shrine|ja|開口神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaikō Shrine|1=Aguchi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11655187}} as many as 13 taiko platforms and at {{ill|Sugawara Shrine (Sakai)|ja|菅原神社 (堺市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara Shrine (Sakai)|1=Sugawara Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11619211}} as many as 14 were offered, marking the golden age of Sakai’s futon taiko. Each district expressed its individuality through unique decorative features—such as tassels in colors other than white, embroidered futon coverings (for example, Shinzaike-hama still uses blue), and varied tying methods. In the {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|qid=Q327567|lt=Shōwa period|1=Shōwa era}}, due to labor shortages and other circumstances, some parishioners began relinquishing their taiko platforms, and later many futon taiko were lost in the {{ill|Sakai Air Raid|ja|堺大空襲|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakai Air Raid|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大阪大空襲|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大阪大空襲|1=Bombing of Osaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q2814395}}. In the postwar period, because of material shortages, decoration reverted to a basic style; however, in recent years there has been a revival toward the lavish prewar style. Since 1974, futon taiko have participated in the {{ill|Sakai Matsuri|en|Sakai Matsuri|qid=Q11428095|lt=Sakai Festival|1=Sakai Matsuri}} and its eve festival. Initially, only those from Ashiharahama, Dainantogawa, Kitatogawa, and Shinzaike-hama participated; later, 10 district groups joined. All these taiko platforms are members of the {{ill|Sakai Festival Futon Taiko Preservation Union|ja|堺まつりふとん太鼓連合保存会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakai Festival Futon Taiko Preservation Union|1=Sakai Festival Futon Taiko Preservation Union|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11428096}}. * Note: Ashiharahama, Dejima, Nishiminato, and Higashiminato withdrew in 2019. In 1991, Ashiharahama; in 1993, Dejima; and in 2002, the Ishizutsukawamukai (specifically Ishizuwakanaka) represented the union in the {{ill|Midosuji Parade|ja|御堂筋パレード|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Midosuji Parade|1=Midosuji Parade|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11488902}}. On May 1, 2019, a commemorative ceremony was held at {{ill|Daisen Park|ja|大仙公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisen Park|qq=}}, centered on the Sakai Festival Futon Taiko Preservation Union, to mark the accession of the new Emperor. Eleven taiko platforms (from Ishizutsukawamukai, Enoki, Dainantogawa, Enokicho, Kaishenhama, Kitatogawa, Shinzaike-hama, Daikōhama, Nishiminato, Hamaishi, and Higashiminato) participated. The ceremony also included participation from districts (such as Enokicho, Kaishenhama, and Hamaishi) that do not normally participate in the Sakai Festival. Due to the impact of the {{ill|Novel coronavirus|en|Novel coronavirus|qid=Q84497971|lt=novel coronavirus|1=Novel coronavirus}}, in 2020 and 2021 the parading of taiko platforms was suspended in various regions. == Chants and Shouts == 【Carrying Call】 "Yoitoyaiyamaka, Dokkoisa-no-se (Sōryā – dekita! Dekita!)" 【Festival Song】 *Verse 1* "Autumn moon over {{ill|Ishiyama|ja|石山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishiyama|1=Ishiyama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q15890429}}, Clouds cluster and blossoms ride upon the wind; News comes on the breeze from the island of {{ill|Awa|ja|阿波|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Awa|1=Q11657489|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11657489}}, In a striped wallet, five or ten ryo; What is that rumbling sound? After earthquakes and thunder, the evening shower comes— Berabera, berashoshoshoi!" *Verse 2* "Peonies with Chinese lion motifs, Bamboo chased by tigers— In pursuit of the noble one, lending wisdom; At {{ill|Chūzan Seikan-ji|ja|中山清閑寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūzan Seikan-ji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135490861|1=Seikan-ji Temple}}, The resident is a monk, Thus it is the Hassaku rain— Berabera, berashoshoshoi!" *(In some renditions the line “Thus it is the Hassaku rain” is omitted.)* *Verse 3* *(Once sung mainly in the old city district; now preserved only at select sites such as Kaishenhama.)* In addition, many districts have their own original festival songs. 【Victory Shout】 "Yāe, Yāe, Āyoiyamakasokojaina, Yōiyase!" == Preserved Taiko Platforms == In {{ill|Sakai-ku, Sakai City|ja|堺市堺区|1=Sakai-ku, Sakai|2=ja|3=堺区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakai-ku, Sakai City|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1042008}} – at the {{ill|Sakai City Museum|en|Sakai City Museum|qid=Q1448918|lt=Sakai City Museum|1=Sakai City Museum}} * Honchō (Predecessor generation) In {{ill|Sakai-ku, Sakai City|ja|堺市堺区|1=Sakai-ku, Sakai|2=ja|3=堺区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakai-ku, Sakai City|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1042008}}, within the warehouse on the grounds of {{ill|Gekkō Shrine|ja|月洲神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gekkō Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135490862|1=Tsukisu Shrine}} in {{ill|Minamishimamachi|ja|南嶋町|comment=I know this is not a translation but it is actually true and I think this might be not a real location|1=Sakai|2=ja|3=堺市|qid=Q193428|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamishimamachi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * Namimachi (abolished) Note: Not open to the public. In Kita-Tabori-chō, Sakai City – inside the compound of {{ill|Sugawara Shrine|ja|菅原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara Shrine|qq=}} at Nan-dōgahama * Kai (Kitakai) (suspended) Note: Not open to the public. == Abolished Futon Taiko == Due to air raids during the {{ill|World War II|en|World War II|qid=Q362|lt=Second World War|1=World War II}} and subsequent carrier shortages, many taiko platforms were abolished. At its peak in the old city district, there were roughly 30 district groups’ worth of futon taiko—comparable to the total number currently in Sakai City. (Old City District) {{ill|Sugawara Shrine|ja|菅原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara Shrine|qq=}} * Kitajima * Kitahama * Kunigahama * Shakurimono * Yadohama * Ueda * Namimatsu * Kai/Kitakai * Minatogawa * (Others) {{ill|Kaikō Shrine|ja|開口神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaikō Shrine|1=Aguchi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11655187}} * Ōhama * Kenchū-dai (or Daikumi-dai) * Konya-hama * (Various groups) * Daikōhama (revived in {{ill|1998|en|1998|qid=Q2089|lt=Heisei 10|1=1998}}) * Nakadohama * Nakategawa * Higashidaikō * (Others) {{ill|Katagae Shrine|ja|方違神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katagae Shrine|1=Katamigae Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11504380}} * Kawaramachi * Hanadaguchi {{ill|Gekkō Shrine|ja|月洲神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gekkō Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135490862|1=Tsukisu Shrine}} * Minashima {{ill|Tamorikami Shrine|ja|田守神社|1=Tamorino Shrine|2=ja|3=田守神社|qid=Q135039249|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamorikami Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * Matsuya {{ill|Nonomiya Shrine|en|Nonomiya Shrine|qid=Q501266|lt=Nonomiya Shrine|1=Nonomiya Shrine}} * Nakanoshinden (later transferred to floats; now in Higashimachi) * Hatayama (transferred to floats; now in Hatayama-chō) == Taiko Platforms in Kaizuka City == '''''{{ill|Kanda Shrine|ja|感田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda Shrine|qq=}} Summer Festival (Adult Taiko Platforms) • Autumn Festival (Child Taiko)''''' [[File:Night in Kaizuka Taiko Platform.jpg|thumb|A Kaizuka taiko platform decorated with lanterns. In recent years, the South Taiko has also adopted a light-up method from {{ill|2017|en|2017|qid=Q25290|lt=Heisei 29|1=2017}}; however, from a traditional viewpoint some oppose imitating other regions. In the second year, the evening uses “lanterns” while the main shrine uses “light-up.”|alt=|left|283x283px]] [[File:0minaminisi.jpg|thumb|In {{ill|Taishō 8|ja|大正八年|1=1919|2=ja|3=1919年|lt=Taishō 8|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishō 8|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q2157}} at the main shrine of {{ill|Kaizuka Hama|ja|貝塚浜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaizuka Hama|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a newly refurbished West Taiko Platform is shown (note that the belt is initially white and turns black over subsequent years). This is a masterpiece by {{ill|Yoshikuni Sakurai|ja|桜井義国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikuni Sakurai|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=DELETED_QID|1=Q135493131}}, executed with full vigor. The left platform is the lost predecessor South Taiko (said to have been burned in a warehouse fire due to a plague outbreak).|alt=|left|286x286px]] The Hassaku Festival at {{ill|Kaizuka Shrine|ja|感田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaizuka Shrine|1=Kanda Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11494969}} (officially “{{ill|Kaizuka Miya Taiko Platform Festival|ja|貝塚宮太鼓台祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaizuka Miya Taiko Platform Festival|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}”) is held on the Friday immediately preceding Golden Day, Saturday, and Sunday. In {{ill|泉州 (disambiguation)|en|泉州 (disambiguation)|qid=Q11143129|lt=Senshū|1=泉州 (disambiguation)}}, the original Hassaku Festival featured futon taiko even before their formal dedication, with a history of about 600 years.
