Due to the impact of the {{ill|Novel coronavirus|en|Novel coronavirus|qid=Q84497971|lt=novel coronavirus|1=Novel coronavirus}}, in 2020 and 2021 the parading of taiko platforms was suspended in various regions. == Chants and Shouts == 【Carrying Call】 "Yoitoyaiyamaka, Dokkoisa-no-se (Sōryā – dekita! Dekita!)" 【Festival Song】 *Verse 1* "Autumn moon over {{ill|Ishiyama|ja|石山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishiyama|1=Ishiyama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q15890429}}, Clouds cluster and blossoms ride on the wind; News travels on the breeze from the island of {{ill|Awa|ja|阿波|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Awa|1=Q11657489|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11657489}}, In a striped wallet, five or ten ryo; What is that rumbling sound? After earthquakes and thunder, the evening shower comes— Berabera, berashoshoshoi!" *Verse 2* "Peonies with Chinese lion motifs, Bamboo pursued by tigers— Chasing the noble one, lending wisdom; At {{ill|Chūzan Seikan-ji|ja|中山清閑寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūzan Seikan-ji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135490861|1=Seikan-ji Temple}}, The resident is a monk, Thus it is the Hassaku rain— Berabera, berashoshoshoi!" *(In some renditions the line “Thus it is the Hassaku rain” is omitted.)* *Verse 3* *(Once sung mainly in the old city district; now preserved only at select sites such as Kaishenhama.)* In addition, many districts have their own original festival songs. 【Victory Shout】 "Yāe, Yāe, Āyoiyamakasokojaina, Yōiyase!" == Preserved Taiko Platforms == In {{ill|Sakai-ku, Sakai City|ja|堺市堺区|1=Sakai-ku, Sakai|2=ja|3=堺区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakai-ku, Sakai City|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1042008}} – at the {{ill|Sakai City Museum|en|Sakai City Museum|qid=Q1448918|lt=Sakai City Museum|1=Sakai City Museum}} * Honchō (Predecessor generation) In {{ill|Sakai-ku, Sakai City|ja|堺市堺区|1=Sakai-ku, Sakai|2=ja|3=堺区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakai-ku, Sakai City|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1042008}}, within the warehouse on the grounds of {{ill|Gekkō Shrine|ja|月洲神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gekkō Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135490862|1=Tsukisu Shrine}} in {{ill|Minamishimamachi|ja|南嶋町|comment=I know this is not a translation but it is actually true and I think this might be not a real location|1=Sakai|2=ja|3=堺市|qid=Q193428|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamishimamachi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * Namimachi (abolished) Note: Not open to the public. In Kita-Tabori-chō, Sakai City – inside the compound of {{ill|Sugawara Shrine|ja|菅原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara Shrine|qq=}} at Nan-dōgahama * Kai (Kitakai) (suspended) Note: Not open to the public. == Abolished Futon Taiko == Due to air raids during the {{ill|World War II|en|World War II|qid=Q362|lt=Second World War|1=World War II}} and subsequent carrier shortages, many taiko platforms were abolished. At its peak in the old city district, there were roughly 30 district groups’ worth of futon taiko—comparable to the total number currently in Sakai City. (Old City District) {{ill|Sugawara Shrine|ja|菅原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara Shrine|qq=}} * Kitajima * Kitahama * Kunigahama * Shakurimono * Yadohama * Ueda * Namimatsu * Kai/Kitakai * Minatogawa * (Others) {{ill|Kaikō Shrine|ja|開口神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaikō Shrine|1=Aguchi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11655187}} * Ōhama * Kenchū-dai (or Daikumi-dai) * Konya-hama * (Various groups) * Daikōhama (revived in {{ill|1998|en|1998|qid=Q2089|lt=Heisei 10|1=1998}}) * Nakadohama * Nakategawa * Higashidaikō * (Others) {{ill|Katagae Shrine|ja|方違神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katagae Shrine|1=Katamigae Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11504380}} * Kawaramachi * Hanadaguchi {{ill|Gekkō Shrine|ja|月洲神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gekkō Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135490862|1=Tsukisu Shrine}} * Minashima {{ill|Tamorikami Shrine|ja|田守神社|1=Tamorino Shrine|2=ja|3=田守神社|qid=Q135039249|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamorikami Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * Matsuya {{ill|Nonomiya Shrine|en|Nonomiya Shrine|qid=Q501266|lt=Nonomiya Shrine|1=Nonomiya Shrine}} * Nakanoshinden (later transferred to floats; now in Higashimachi) * Hatayama (transferred to floats; now in Hatayama-chō) == Taiko Platforms in Kaizuka City == '''''{{ill|Kanda Shrine|ja|感田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda Shrine|qq=}} Summer Festival (Adult Taiko Platforms) • Autumn Festival (Child Taiko)''''' [[File:Night in Kaizuka Taiko Platform.jpg|thumb|A Kaizuka taiko platform decorated with lanterns. In recent years, the South Taiko has also adopted a light-up method from {{ill|2017|en|2017|qid=Q25290|lt=Heisei 29|1=2017}}; however, from a traditional viewpoint some oppose imitating other regions. In the second year, the evening uses “lanterns” while the main shrine uses “light-up.”|alt=|left|283x283px]] [[File:0minaminisi.jpg|thumb|In {{ill|Taishō 8|ja|大正八年|1=1919|2=ja|3=1919年|lt=Taishō 8|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishō 8|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q2157}} at the main shrine of {{ill|Kaizuka Hama|ja|貝塚浜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaizuka Hama|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a newly refurbished West Taiko Platform is shown (note that the belt is initially white and turns black over subsequent years). This is a masterpiece by {{ill|Yoshikuni Sakurai|ja|桜井義国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikuni Sakurai|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=DELETED_QID|1=Q135493131}}, executed with full vigor. The left platform is the lost predecessor South Taiko (said to have been burned in a warehouse fire due to a plague outbreak).|alt=|left|286x286px]] The Hassaku Festival at {{ill|Kaizuka Shrine|ja|感田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaizuka Shrine|1=Kanda Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11494969}} (officially “{{ill|Kaizuka Miya Taiko Platform Festival|ja|貝塚宮太鼓台祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaizuka Miya Taiko Platform Festival|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}”) is held on the Friday immediately preceding Golden Day, Saturday, and Sunday. In {{ill|泉州 (disambiguation)|en|泉州 (disambiguation)|qid=Q11143129|lt=Senshū|1=泉州 (disambiguation)}}, the original Hassaku Festival featured futon taiko even before their formal dedication, with a history of about 600 years. At its peak, 13 taiko platforms were paraded. * Ashiharahama * Dainantogawa/Sumida (Sakai-type) * Shinzaike-hama * Daikōhama (Note: At one time, Daikōhama was converted to a futon taiko with red and white futon during its 10th-anniversary refurbishment.) * Additionally, child taiko from Minamihanmachi participate. * Daikōhama was revived after roughly 70 years by local volunteers. * From September 8, 2024, the Dainantogawa Sumida Youth Association has been renamed the Dainantogawa Sumida Taiko Association. '''{{ill|Sugawara Shrine (Sakai)|ja|菅原神社 (堺市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara Shrine (Sakai)|1=Sugawara Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11619211}} Hassaku Festival''' (held on the Friday immediately preceding Golden Day, Saturday, and Sunday) * {{ill|Kaishenhama|ja|海船濱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaishenhama|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Sakai-type) * {{ill|Kitatogawa|ja|北戸川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitatogawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Sakai-type) Child taiko from Kitatogawa also participate. * Until 2017 ({{ill|2017|en|2017|qid=Q25290|lt=Heisei 29|1=2017}}) taiko platforms were typically paraded on September 13–15; however, due to lower turnout on weekdays compared to holidays, from 2018 ({{ill|2018|en|2018|qid=Q25291|lt=Heisei 30|1=2018}}) the schedule was changed to match that of the Kaikō Shrine Hassaku Festival (Friday preceding Golden Day, Saturday, and Sunday). * Before World War II, as many as 14 taiko platforms were paraded at the peak; subsequently, many were abolished due to air raids and carrier shortages, and currently only 2 are paraded. * In addition to futon taiko, Kitatogawa also features a lion dance called “{{ill|Togawa no Shishi|ja|戸川之獅子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Togawa no Shishi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}” which, aside from the Hassaku Festival, also appears at local Bon festivals and in park or school events. == (Additional sections follow for taiko platforms in other cities such as Izumisano, Ibaraki, Nara, and further regions; as well as sections on history, chants, preserved and abolished platforms, and related external links.) == == History == The futon taiko of the former {{ill|Sakai|en|Sakai|qid=Q193428|lt=Sakai City|1=Sakai}} are said to have been carried as early as the mid-Edo period during the festivals of {{ill|Sumiyoshi-taisha|en|Sumiyoshi-taisha|qid=Q705949|lt=Sumiyoshi Taisha|1=Sumiyoshi-taisha}}, as depicted on the “{{nihongo|Mimura Miyamatsuri Ema|ja|三村宮祭礼絵馬|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mimura Miyamatsuri Ema|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.” In Sakai City, until roughly the mid-Meiji period, each local parishioner group would pull floats and halberds during festivals, and taiko platforms were integral to the ritual. However, on a festival held on August 1 (old calendar) in {{ill|1896|en|1896|qid=Q7846|lt=Meiji 29|1=1896}}, on a narrow section of the {{ill|Kishu Highway|ja|紀州街道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kishu Highway|1=Kishū Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11605050}} in Nakanomachi West (narrow enough for only one float), a collision occurred between a boat float from the Minato group and a float from the northern blacksmith district, resulting in a dispute and casualties. This incident, known as the “Sakai Float Incident,” led to a complete ban on parading elaborately decorated floats in the old Sakai area thereafter. Later, to celebrate Japan’s victory in the {{ill|Russo-Japanese War|en|Russo-Japanese War|qid=Q159950|lt=Russo-Japanese War|1=Russo-Japanese War}}, the parading of elaborately decorated floats was permitted again; however, because parishioners disposed of or sold their floats, many float craftsmen left Sakai or changed trades, and concerns over safety—and the fact that many regions began purchasing futon taiko from {{ill|Awaji|en|Awaji|qid=Q349322|lt=Awaji|1=Awaji}}—resulted in only futon taiko remaining. In the {{ill|Taishō period|ja|大正時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishō period|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大正|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大正|1=Taishō era|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q329174}}, at {{ill|Kaikō Shrine|ja|開口神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaikō Shrine|1=Aguchi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11655187}} as many as 13 taiko platforms and at {{ill|Sugawara Shrine (Sakai)|ja|菅原神社 (堺市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara Shrine (Sakai)|1=Sugawara Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11619211}} as many as 14 were offered, marking the golden age of Sakai’s futon taiko. Each district expressed its individuality through unique decorative features—such as tassels in colors other than white, embroidered futon coverings (for example, Shinzaike-hama still uses blue), and varied tying methods. In the {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|qid=Q327567|lt=Shōwa period|1=Shōwa era}}, due to labor shortages and other circumstances, some parishioners began relinquishing their taiko platforms, and later many futon taiko were lost in the {{ill|Sakai Air Raid|ja|堺大空襲|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakai Air Raid|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大阪大空襲|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大阪大空襲|1=Bombing of Osaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q2814395}}. In the postwar period, because of material shortages, decoration reverted to a basic style; however, in recent years there has been a revival toward the lavish prewar style. Since 1974, futon taiko have participated in the {{ill|Sakai Matsuri|en|Sakai Matsuri|qid=Q11428095|lt=Sakai Festival|1=Sakai Matsuri}} and its eve festival. Initially, only those from Ashiharahama, Dainantogawa, Kitatogawa, and Shinzaike-hama participated; later, 10 district groups joined. All these taiko platforms are members of the {{ill|Sakai Festival Futon Taiko Preservation Union|ja|堺まつりふとん太鼓連合保存会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakai Festival Futon Taiko Preservation Union|1=Sakai Festival Futon Taiko Preservation Union|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11428096}}. * Note: Ashiharahama, Dejima, Nishiminato, and Higashiminato withdrew in 2019. In 1991, Ashiharahama; in 1993, Dejima; and in 2002, the Ishizutsukawamukai (specifically Ishizuwakanaka) represented the union in the {{ill|Midosuji Parade|ja|御堂筋パレード|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Midosuji Parade|1=Midosuji Parade|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11488902}}. On May 1, 2019, a commemorative ceremony was held at {{ill|Daisen Park|ja|大仙公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisen Park|qq=}}, centered on the Sakai Festival Futon Taiko Preservation Union, to mark the accession of the new Emperor. Eleven taiko platforms (from Ishizutsukawamukai, Enoki, Dainantogawa, Enokicho, Kaishenhama, Kitatogawa, Shinzaike-hama, Daikōhama, Nishiminato, Hamaishi, and Higashiminato) participated. The ceremony also saw participation from districts (such as Enokicho, Kaishenhama, and Hamaishi) that do not normally participate in the Sakai Festival. Due to the impact of the {{ill|Novel coronavirus|en|Novel coronavirus|qid=Q84497971|lt=novel coronavirus|1=Novel coronavirus}}, in 2020 and 2021 the parading of taiko platforms was suspended in various regions. == Chants and Shouts == 【Carrying Call】 "Yoitoyaiyamaka, Dokkoisa-no-se (Sōryā – dekita! however, due to lower turnout on weekdays compared to holidays, from 2018 ({{ill|2018|en|2018|qid=Q25291|lt=Heisei 30|1=2018}}) the schedule was changed to match that of the Kaikō Shrine Hassaku Festival (Friday preceding Golden Day, Saturday, and Sunday). * Before World War II, as many as 14 taiko platforms were paraded at the peak; subsequently, many were abolished due to air raids and a shortage of carriers, and currently only 2 are paraded. * In addition to futon taiko, Kitatogawa also features a lion dance called “{{ill|Togawa no Shishi|ja|戸川之獅子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Togawa no Shishi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}” which, aside from the Hassaku Festival, also appears at local Bon festivals and in park or school events. == (Additional sections follow for taiko platforms in other cities such as Izumisano, Ibaraki, Nara, and further regions; as well as sections on history, chants, preserved and abolished platforms, and related external links.) == == History == The futon taiko of the former {{ill|Sakai|en|Sakai|qid=Q193428|lt=Sakai City|1=Sakai}} are said to have been carried as early as the mid-Edo period during the festivals of {{ill|Sumiyoshi-taisha|en|Sumiyoshi-taisha|qid=Q705949|lt=Sumiyoshi Taisha|1=Sumiyoshi-taisha}}, as depicted on the “{{nihongo|Mimura Miyamatsuri Ema|ja|三村宮祭礼絵馬|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mimura Miyamatsuri Ema|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.” In Sakai City, until roughly the mid-Meiji period, each local parishioner group would pull floats and halberds during festivals, and taiko platforms were integral to the ritual. However, on a festival held on August 1 (old calendar) in {{ill|1896|en|1896|qid=Q7846|lt=Meiji 29|1=1896}}, on a narrow section of the {{ill|Kishu Highway|ja|紀州街道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kishu Highway|1=Kishū Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11605050}} in Nakanomachi West (narrow enough for only one float), a collision occurred between a boat float from the Minato group and a float from the northern blacksmith district, resulting in a dispute and casualties. This incident, known as the “Sakai Float Incident,” led to a complete ban on parading elaborately decorated floats in the old Sakai area thereafter. Later, to celebrate Japan’s victory in the {{ill|Russo-Japanese War|en|Russo-Japanese War|qid=Q159950|lt=Russo-Japanese War|1=Russo-Japanese War}}, the parading of elaborately decorated floats was permitted again; however, because parishioners disposed of or sold their floats, many float craftsmen left Sakai or changed trades, and concerns over safety—and the fact that many regions began purchasing futon taiko from {{ill|Awaji|en|Awaji|qid=Q349322|lt=Awaji|1=Awaji}}—resulted in only futon taiko remaining. In the {{ill|Taishō period|ja|大正時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishō period|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大正|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大正|1=Taishō era|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q329174}}, at {{ill|Kaikō Shrine|ja|開口神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaikō Shrine|1=Aguchi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11655187}} as many as 13 taiko platforms and at {{ill|Sugawara Shrine (Sakai)|ja|菅原神社 (堺市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara Shrine (Sakai)|1=Sugawara Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11619211}} as many as 14 were offered, marking the golden age of Sakai’s futon taiko. Each district expressed its individuality through unique decorative features—such as tassels in colors other than white, embroidered futon coverings (for example, Shinzaike-hama still uses blue), and varied tying methods. In the {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|qid=Q327567|lt=Shōwa period|1=Shōwa era}}, due to labor shortages and other circumstances, some parishioners began relinquishing their taiko platforms, and later many futon taiko were lost in the {{ill|Sakai Air Raid|ja|堺大空襲|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakai Air Raid|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大阪大空襲|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大阪大空襲|1=Bombing of Osaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q2814395}}. In the postwar period, because of material shortages, decoration reverted to a basic style; however, in recent years there has been a revival toward the lavish prewar style. Since 1974, futon taiko have participated in the {{ill|Sakai Matsuri|en|Sakai Matsuri|qid=Q11428095|lt=Sakai Festival|1=Sakai Matsuri}} and its eve festival. Initially, only those from Ashiharahama, Dainantogawa, Kitatogawa, and Shinzaike-hama participated; later, 10 district groups joined. All these taiko platforms are members of the {{ill|Sakai Festival Futon Taiko Preservation Union|ja|堺まつりふとん太鼓連合保存会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakai Festival Futon Taiko Preservation Union|1=Sakai Festival Futon Taiko Preservation Union|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11428096}}. * Note: Ashiharahama, Dejima, Nishiminato, and Higashiminato withdrew in 2019. In 1991, Ashiharahama; in 1993, Dejima; and in 2002, the Ishizutsukawamukai (specifically Ishizuwakanaka) represented the union in the {{ill|Midosuji Parade|ja|御堂筋パレード|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Midosuji Parade|1=Midosuji Parade|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11488902}}. On May 1, 2019, a commemorative ceremony was held at {{ill|Daisen Park|ja|大仙公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisen Park|qq=}}, centered on the Sakai Festival Futon Taiko Preservation Union, to mark the accession of the new Emperor. Eleven taiko platforms (from Ishizutsukawamukai, Enoki, Dainantogawa, Enokicho, Kaishenhama, Kitatogawa, Shinzaike-hama, Daikōhama, Nishiminato, Hamaishi, and Higashiminato) participated. The ceremony also saw participation from districts (such as Enokicho, Kaishenhama, and Hamaishi) that do not normally participate in the Sakai Festival. Due to the impact of the {{ill|Novel coronavirus|en|Novel coronavirus|qid=Q84497971|lt=novel coronavirus|1=Novel coronavirus}}, in 2020 and 2021 the parading of taiko platforms was suspended in various regions. == Chants and Shouts == 【Carrying Call】 "Yoitoyaiyamaka, Dokkoisa-no-se (Sōryā – dekita! Dekita!)" 【Festival Song】 *Verse 1* "Autumn moon over {{ill|Ishiyama|ja|石山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishiyama|1=Ishiyama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q15890429}}, Clouds cluster and blossoms ride upon the wind; News travels on the breeze from the island of {{ill|Awa|ja|阿波|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Awa|1=Q11657489|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11657489}}, In a striped wallet, five or ten ryo; What is that rumbling sound? After earthquakes and thunder, the evening shower comes— Berabera, berashoshoshoi!" *Verse 2* "Peonies with Chinese lion motifs, Bamboo pursued by tigers— Chasing the noble one, lending wisdom; At {{ill|Chūzan Seikan-ji|ja|中山清閑寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūzan Seikan-ji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135490861|1=Seikan-ji Temple}}, The resident is a monk, Thus it is the Hassaku rain— Berabera, berashoshoshoi!" *(In some renditions the line “Thus it is the Hassaku rain” is omitted.)* *Verse 3* *(Once sung mainly in the old city district; now preserved only at select sites such as Kaishenhama.)* In addition, many districts have their own original festival songs. 【Victory Shout】 "Yāe, Yāe, Āyoiyamakasokojaina, Yōiyase!" == Preserved Taiko Platforms == In {{ill|Sakai-ku, Sakai City|ja|堺市堺区|1=Sakai-ku, Sakai|2=ja|3=堺区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakai-ku, Sakai City|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1042008}} – at the {{ill|Sakai City Museum|en|Sakai City Museum|qid=Q1448918|lt=Sakai City Museum|1=Sakai City Museum}} * Honchō (Predecessor generation) In {{ill|Sakai-ku, Sakai City|ja|堺市堺区|1=Sakai-ku, Sakai|2=ja|3=堺区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakai-ku, Sakai City|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1042008}}, within the warehouse on the grounds of {{ill|Gekkō Shrine|ja|月洲神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gekkō Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135490862|1=Tsukisu Shrine}} in {{ill|Minamishimamachi|ja|南嶋町|comment=I know this is not a translation but it is actually true and I think this might be not a real location|1=Sakai|2=ja|3=堺市|qid=Q193428|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamishimamachi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * Namimachi (abolished) Note: Not open to the public. In Kita-Tabori-chō, Sakai City – inside the compound of {{ill|Sugawara Shrine|ja|菅原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara Shrine|qq=}} at Nan-dōgahama * Kai (Kitakai) (suspended) Note: Not open to the public. == Abolished Futon Taiko == Due to air raids during the {{ill|World War II|en|World War II|qid=Q362|lt=Second World War|1=World War II}} and subsequent carrier shortages, many taiko platforms were abolished. At its peak in the old city district, there were roughly 30 district groups’ worth of futon taiko—comparable to the total number currently in Sakai City. (Old City District) {{ill|Sugawara Shrine|ja|菅原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara Shrine|qq=}} * Kitajima * Kitahama * Kunigahama * Shakurimono * Yadohama * Ueda * Namimatsu * Kai/Kitakai * Minatogawa * (Others) {{ill|Kaikō Shrine|ja|開口神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaikō Shrine|1=Aguchi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11655187}} * Ōhama * Kenchū-dai (or Daikumi-dai) * Konya-hama * (Various groups) * Daikōhama (revived in {{ill|1998|en|1998|qid=Q2089|lt=Heisei 10|1=1998}}) * Nakadohama * Nakategawa * Higashidaikō * (Others) {{ill|Katagae Shrine|ja|方違神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katagae Shrine|1=Katamigae Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11504380}} * Kawaramachi * Hanadaguchi {{ill|Gekkō Shrine|ja|月洲神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gekkō Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135490862|1=Tsukisu Shrine}} * Minashima {{ill|Tamorikami Shrine|ja|田守神社|1=Tamorino Shrine|2=ja|3=田守神社|qid=Q135039249|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamorikami Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * Matsuya {{ill|Nonomiya Shrine|en|Nonomiya Shrine|qid=Q501266|lt=Nonomiya Shrine|1=Nonomiya Shrine}} * Nakanoshinden (later transferred to floats; now in Higashimachi) * Hatayama (transferred to floats; now in Hatayama-chō) == Taiko Platforms in Kaizuka City == '''''{{ill|Kanda Shrine|ja|感田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda Shrine|qq=}} Summer Festival (Adult Taiko Platforms) • Autumn Festival (Child Taiko)''''' [[File:Night in Kaizuka Taiko Platform.jpg|thumb|A Kaizuka taiko platform decorated with lanterns. In recent years, the South Taiko has also adopted a light-up method from {{ill|2017|en|2017|qid=Q25290|lt=Heisei 29|1=2017}}; however, from a traditional viewpoint some oppose imitating other regions. In the second year, the evening uses “lanterns” while the main shrine uses “light-up.”|alt=|left|283x283px]] [[File:0minaminisi.jpg|thumb|In {{ill|Taishō 8|ja|大正八年|1=1919|2=ja|3=1919年|lt=Taishō 8|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishō 8|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q2157}} at the main shrine of {{ill|Kaizuka Hama|ja|貝塚浜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaizuka Hama|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a newly refurbished West Taiko Platform is shown (note that the belt is initially white and turns black over subsequent years). This is a masterpiece by {{ill|Yoshikuni Sakurai|ja|桜井義国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikuni Sakurai|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=DELETED_QID|1=Q135493131}}, executed with full vigor. The left platform is the lost predecessor South Taiko (said to have been burned in a warehouse fire due to a plague outbreak).|alt=|left|286x286px]] The Hassaku Festival at {{ill|Kaizuka Shrine|ja|感田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaizuka Shrine|1=Kanda Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11494969}} (officially “{{ill|Kaizuka Miya Taiko Platform Festival|ja|貝塚宮太鼓台祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaizuka Miya Taiko Platform Festival|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}”) is held on the Friday immediately preceding Golden Day, Saturday, and Sunday. In {{ill|泉州 (disambiguation)|en|泉州 (disambiguation)|qid=Q11143129|lt=Senshū|1=泉州 (disambiguation)}}, the original Hassaku Festival featured futon taiko even before their formal dedication, with a history of about 600 years. At its peak, 13 taiko platforms were paraded. * Ashiharahama * Dainantogawa/Sumida (Sakai-type) * Shinzaike-hama * Daikōhama (Note: At one time, Daikōhama was converted to a futon taiko with red and white futon during its 10th-anniversary refurbishment.) * Additionally, child taiko from Minamihanmachi participate. * Daikōhama was revived after roughly 70 years by local volunteers. * From September 8, 2024, the Dainantogawa Sumida Youth Association has been renamed the Dainantogawa Sumida Taiko Association. '''{{ill|Sugawara Shrine (Sakai)|ja|菅原神社 (堺市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara Shrine (Sakai)|1=Sugawara Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11619211}} Hassaku Festival''' (held on the Friday immediately preceding Golden Day, Saturday, and Sunday) * {{ill|Kaishenhama|ja|海船濱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaishenhama|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Sakai-type) * {{ill|Kitatogawa|ja|北戸川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitatogawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Sakai-type) Child taiko from Kitatogawa also participate. * Until 2017 ({{ill|2017|en|2017|qid=Q25290|lt=Heisei 29|1=2017}}) taiko platforms were typically paraded on September 13–15; however, due to lower turnout on weekdays compared to holidays, from 2018 ({{ill|2018|en|2018|qid=Q25291|lt=Heisei 30|1=2018}}) the schedule was changed to match that of the Kaikō Shrine Hassaku Festival (Friday preceding Golden Day, Saturday, and Sunday). * Before World War II, as many as 14 taiko platforms were paraded at the peak; subsequently, many were abolished due to air raids and a shortage of carriers, and currently only 2 are paraded. * In addition to futon taiko, Kitatogawa also features a lion dance called “{{ill|Togawa no Shishi|ja|戸川之獅子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Togawa no Shishi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}” which, aside from the Hassaku Festival, also appears at local Bon festivals and in park or school events. == (Additional sections follow for taiko platforms in other cities such as Izumisano, Ibaraki, Nara, and further regions; as well as sections on history, chants, preserved and abolished platforms, and related external links.) == == History == The futon taiko of the former {{ill|Sakai|en|Sakai|qid=Q193428|lt=Sakai City|1=Sakai}} are said to have been carried as early as the mid-Edo period during the festivals of {{ill|Sumiyoshi-taisha|en|Sumiyoshi-taisha|qid=Q705949|lt=Sumiyoshi Taisha|1=Sumiyoshi-taisha}}, as depicted on the “{{nihongo|Mimura Miyamatsuri Ema|ja|三村宮祭礼絵馬|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mimura Miyamatsuri Ema|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.” In Sakai City, until roughly the mid-Meiji period, each local parishioner group would pull floats and halberds during festivals, and taiko platforms were integral to the ritual. However, on a festival held on August 1 (old calendar) in {{ill|1896|en|1896|qid=Q7846|lt=Meiji 29|1=1896}}, on a narrow section of the {{ill|Kishu Highway|ja|紀州街道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kishu Highway|1=Kishū Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11605050}} in Nakanomachi West (narrow enough for only one float), a collision occurred between a boat float from the Minato group and a float from the northern blacksmith district, resulting in a dispute and casualties. This incident, known as the “Sakai Float Incident,” led to a complete ban on parading elaborately decorated floats in the old Sakai area thereafter. Later, to celebrate Japan’s victory in the {{ill|Russo-Japanese War|en|Russo-Japanese War|qid=Q159950|lt=Russo-Japanese War|1=Russo-Japanese War}}, the parading of elaborately decorated floats was permitted again; however, because parishioners disposed of or sold their floats, many float craftsmen left Sakai or changed trades, and concerns over safety—and the fact that many regions began purchasing futon taiko from {{ill|Awaji|en|Awaji|qid=Q349322|lt=Awaji|1=Awaji}}—resulted in only futon taiko remaining. Each district expressed its individuality through unique decorative features—such as tassels in colors other than white, embroidered futon coverings (for example, Shinzaike-hama still uses blue), and varied tying methods. In the {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|qid=Q327567|lt=Shōwa period|1=Shōwa era}}, due to labor shortages and other circumstances, some parishioners began relinquishing their taiko platforms, and later many futon taiko were lost in the {{ill|Sakai Air Raid|ja|堺大空襲|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakai Air Raid|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大阪大空襲|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大阪大空襲|1=Bombing of Osaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q2814395}}. In the postwar period, because of material shortages, decoration reverted to a basic style; however, in recent years there has been a revival toward the lavish prewar style. Since 1974, futon taiko have participated in the {{ill|Sakai Matsuri|en|Sakai Matsuri|qid=Q11428095|lt=Sakai Festival|1=Sakai Matsuri}} and its eve festival. Initially, only those from Ashiharahama, Dainantogawa, Kitatogawa, and Shinzaike-hama participated; later, 10 district groups joined. In Kita-Tabori-chō, Sakai City – inside the compound of {{ill|Sugawara Shrine|ja|菅原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara Shrine|qq=}} at Nan-dōgahama * Kai (Kitakai) (suspended) Note: Not open to the public. == Abolished Futon Taiko == Due to air raids during the {{ill|World War II|en|World War II|qid=Q362|lt=Second World War|1=World War II}} and subsequent carrier shortages, many taiko platforms were abolished. At its peak in the old city district, there were roughly 30 district groups’ worth of futon taiko—comparable to the total number currently in Sakai City. (Old City District) {{ill|Sugawara Shrine|ja|菅原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara Shrine|qq=}} * Kitajima * Kitahama * Kunigahama * Shakurimono * Yadohama * Ueda * Namimatsu * Kai/Kitakai * Minatogawa * (Others) {{ill|Kaikō Shrine|ja|開口神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaikō Shrine|1=Aguchi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11655187}} * Ōhama * Kenchū-dai (or Daikumi-dai) * Konya-hama * (Various groups) * Daikōhama (revived in {{ill|1998|en|1998|qid=Q2089|lt=Heisei 10|1=1998}}) * Nakadohama * Nakategawa * Higashidaikō * (Others) {{ill|Katagae Shrine|ja|方違神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katagae Shrine|1=Katamigae Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11504380}} * Kawaramachi * Hanadaguchi {{ill|Gekkō Shrine|ja|月洲神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gekkō Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135490862|1=Tsukisu Shrine}} * Minashima {{ill|Tamorikami Shrine|ja|田守神社|1=Tamorino Shrine|2=ja|3=田守神社|qid=Q135039249|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamorikami Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * Matsuya {{ill|Nonomiya Shrine|en|Nonomiya Shrine|qid=Q501266|lt=Nonomiya Shrine|1=Nonomiya Shrine}} * Nakanoshinden (later transferred to floats; now in Higashimachi) * Hatayama (transferred to floats; now in Hatayama-chō) == Taiko Platforms in Kaizuka City == '''''{{ill|Kanda Shrine|ja|感田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanda Shrine|qq=}} Summer Festival (Adult Taiko Platforms) • Autumn Festival (Child Taiko)''''' [[File:Night in Kaizuka Taiko Platform.jpg|thumb|A Kaizuka taiko platform decorated with lanterns. In recent years, the South Taiko has also adopted a light-up method from {{ill|2017|en|2017|qid=Q25290|lt=Heisei 29|1=2017}}; however, from a traditional viewpoint some oppose imitating other regions. In the second year, the evening uses “lanterns” while the main shrine uses “light-up.”|alt=|left|283x283px]] [[File:0minaminisi.jpg|thumb|In {{ill|Taishō 8|ja|大正八年|1=1919|2=ja|3=1919年|lt=Taishō 8|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishō 8|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q2157}} at the main shrine of {{ill|Kaizuka Hama|ja|貝塚浜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaizuka Hama|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a newly refurbished West Taiko Platform is shown (note that the belt is initially white and turns black over subsequent years). This is a masterpiece by {{ill|Yoshikuni Sakurai|ja|桜井義国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikuni Sakurai|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=DELETED_QID|1=Q135493131}}, executed with full vigor. The left platform is the lost predecessor South Taiko (said to have been burned in a warehouse fire due to a plague outbreak).|alt=|left|286x286px]] The Hassaku Festival at {{ill|Kaizuka Shrine|ja|感田神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaizuka Shrine|1=Kanda Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11494969}} (officially “{{ill|Kaizuka Miya Taiko Platform Festival|ja|貝塚宮太鼓台祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaizuka Miya Taiko Platform Festival|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}”) is held on the Friday immediately preceding Golden Day, Saturday, and Sunday. In {{ill|泉州 (disambiguation)|en|泉州 (disambiguation)|qid=Q11143129|lt=Senshū|1=泉州 (disambiguation)}}, the original Hassaku Festival featured futon taiko even before their formal dedication, with a history of about 600 years. At its peak, 13 taiko platforms were paraded. * Ashiharahama * Dainantogawa/Sumida (Sakai-type) * Shinzaike-hama * Daikōhama (Note: At one time, Daikōhama was converted to a futon taiko with red and white futon during its 10th-anniversary refurbishment.) * Additionally, child taiko from Minamihanmachi participate. * Daikōhama was revived after roughly 70 years by local volunteers. * From September 8, 2024, the Dainantogawa Sumida Youth Association has been renamed the Dainantogawa Sumida Taiko Association. '''{{ill|Sugawara Shrine (Sakai)|ja|菅原神社 (堺市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara Shrine (Sakai)|1=Sugawara Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11619211}} Hassaku Festival''' (held on the Friday immediately preceding Golden Day, Saturday, and Sunday) * {{ill|Kaishenhama|ja|海船濱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaishenhama|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Sakai-type) * {{ill|Kitatogawa|ja|北戸川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitatogawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Sakai-type) Child taiko from Kitatogawa also participate. * Until 2017 ({{ill|2017|en|2017|qid=Q25290|lt=Heisei 29|1=2017}}) taiko platforms were typically paraded on September 13–15; however, due to lower turnout on weekdays compared to holidays, from 2018 ({{ill|2018|en|2018|qid=Q25291|lt=Heisei 30|1=2018}}) the schedule was changed to match that of the Kaikō Shrine Hassaku Festival (Friday preceding Golden Day, Saturday, and Sunday). * Before World War II, as many as 14 taiko platforms were paraded at the peak; subsequently, many were abolished due to air raids and a shortage of carriers, and currently only 2 are paraded. * In addition to futon taiko, Kitatogawa also features a lion dance called “{{ill|Togawa no Shishi|ja|戸川之獅子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Togawa no Shishi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}” which, aside from the Hassaku Festival, also appears at local Bon festivals and in park or school events. == (Additional sections follow for taiko platforms in other cities such as Izumisano, Ibaraki, Nara, and further regions; as well as sections on history, chants, preserved and abolished platforms, and related external links.) == == History == The futon taiko of the former {{ill|Sakai|en|Sakai|qid=Q193428|lt=Sakai City|1=Sakai}} are said to have been carried as early as the mid-Edo period during the festivals of {{ill|Sumiyoshi-taisha|en|Sumiyoshi-taisha|qid=Q705949|lt=Sumiyoshi Taisha|1=Sumiyoshi-taisha}}, as depicted on the “{{nihongo|Mimura Miyamatsuri Ema|ja|三村宮祭礼絵馬|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mimura Miyamatsuri Ema|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.” In Sakai City, until roughly the mid-Meiji period, each local parishioner group would pull floats and halberds during festivals, and taiko platforms were integral to the ritual. However, on a festival held on August 1 (old calendar) in {{ill|1896|en|1896|qid=Q7846|lt=Meiji 29|1=1896}}, on a narrow section of the {{ill|Kishu Highway|ja|紀州街道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kishu Highway|1=Kishū Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11605050}} in Nakanomachi West (narrow enough for only one float), a collision occurred between a boat float from the Minato group and a float from the northern blacksmith district, resulting in a dispute and casualties. This incident, known as the “Sakai Float Incident,” led to a complete ban on parading elaborately decorated floats in the old Sakai area thereafter. Later, to celebrate Japan’s victory in the {{ill|Russo-Japanese War|en|Russo-Japanese War|qid=Q159950|lt=Russo-Japanese War|1=Russo-Japanese War}}, the parading of elaborately decorated floats was permitted again; however, because parishioners disposed of or sold their floats, many float craftsmen left Sakai or changed trades, and concerns over safety—and the fact that many regions began purchasing futon taiko from {{ill|Awaji|en|Awaji|qid=Q349322|lt=Awaji|1=Awaji}}—resulted in only futon taiko remaining. In the {{ill|Taishō period|ja|大正時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishō period|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大正|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大正|1=Taishō era|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q329174}}, at {{ill|Kaikō Shrine|ja|開口神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaikō Shrine|1=Aguchi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11655187}} as many as 13 taiko platforms and at {{ill|Sugawara Shrine (Sakai)|ja|菅原神社 (堺市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara Shrine (Sakai)|1=Sugawara Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11619211}} as many as 14 were offered, marking the golden age of Sakai’s futon taiko. Each district expressed its individuality through unique decorative features—such as tassels in colors other than white, embroidered futon coverings (for example, Shinzaike-hama still uses blue), and varied tying methods. In the {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|qid=Q327567|lt=Shōwa period|1=Shōwa era}}, due to labor shortages and other circumstances, some parishioners began relinquishing their taiko platforms, and later many futon taiko were lost in the {{ill|Sakai Air Raid|ja|堺大空襲|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakai Air Raid|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大阪大空襲|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大阪大空襲|1=Bombing of Osaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q2814395}}. In the postwar period, because of material shortages, decoration reverted to a basic style; however, in recent years there has been a revival toward the lavish prewar style. Since 1974, futon taiko have participated in the {{ill|Sakai Matsuri|en|Sakai Matsuri|qid=Q11428095|lt=Sakai Festival|1=Sakai Matsuri}} and its eve festival. Initially, only those from Ashiharahama, Dainantogawa, Kitatogawa, and Shinzaike-hama participated; later, 10 district groups joined. All these taiko platforms are members of the {{ill|Sakai Festival Futon Taiko Preservation Union|ja|堺まつりふとん太鼓連合保存会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakai Festival Futon Taiko Preservation Union|1=Sakai Festival Futon Taiko Preservation Union|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11428096}}. * Note: Ashiharahama, Dejima, Nishiminato, and Higashiminato withdrew in 2019. In 1991, Ashiharahama; in 1993, Dejima; and in 2002, the Ishizutsukawamukai (specifically Ishizuwakanaka) represented the union in the {{ill|Midosuji Parade|ja|御堂筋パレード|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Midosuji Parade|1=Midosuji Parade|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11488902}}. On May 1, 2019, a commemorative ceremony was held at {{ill|Daisen Park|ja|大仙公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisen Park|qq=}}, centered on the Sakai Festival Futon Taiko Preservation Union, to mark the accession of the new Emperor. Eleven taiko platforms (from Ishizutsukawamukai, Enoki, Dainantogawa, Enokicho, Kaishenhama, Kitatogawa, Shinzaike-hama, Daikōhama, Nishiminato, Hamaishi, and Higashiminato) participated. The ceremony also saw participation from districts (such as Enokicho, Kaishenhama, and Hamaishi) that do not normally participate in the Sakai Festival. Due to the impact of the {{ill|Novel coronavirus|en|Novel coronavirus|qid=Q84497971|lt=novel coronavirus|1=Novel coronavirus}}, in 2020 and 2021 the parading of taiko platforms was suspended in various regions. == Chants and Shouts == 【Carrying Call】 "Yoitoyaiyamaka, Dokkoisa-no-se (Sōryā – dekita! Dekita!)" 【Festival Song】 *Verse 1* "Autumn moon over {{ill|Ishiyama|ja|石山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishiyama|1=Ishiyama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q15890429}}, Clouds cluster and blossoms ride upon the wind; News travels on the breeze from the island of {{ill|Awa|ja|阿波|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Awa|1=Q11657489|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11657489}}, In a striped wallet, five or ten ryo; What is that rumbling sound? After earthquakes and thunder, the evening shower comes— Berabera, berashoshoshoi!" *Verse 2* "Peonies with Chinese lion motifs, Bamboo pursued by tigers— Chasing the noble one, lending wisdom; At {{ill|Chūzan Seikan-ji|ja|中山清閑寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūzan Seikan-ji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135490861|1=Seikan-ji Temple}}, The resident is a monk, Thus it is the Hassaku rain— Berabera, berashoshoshoi!" *(In some renditions the line “Thus it is the Hassaku rain” is omitted.)* *Verse 3* *(Once sung mainly in the old city district; now preserved only at select sites such as Kaishenhama.)* In addition, many districts have their own original festival songs. 【Victory Shout】 "Yāe, Yāe, Āyoiyamakasokojaina, Yōiyase!" == Preserved Taiko Platforms == In {{ill|Sakai-ku, Sakai City|ja|堺市堺区|1=Sakai-ku, Sakai|2=ja|3=堺区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakai-ku, Sakai City|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1042008}} – at the {{ill|Sakai City Museum|en|Sakai City Museum|qid=Q1448918|lt=Sakai City Museum|1=Sakai City Museum}} * Honchō (Predecessor generation) In {{ill|Sakai-ku, Sakai City|ja|堺市堺区|1=Sakai-ku, Sakai|2=ja|3=堺区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakai-ku, Sakai City|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1042008}}, within the warehouse on the grounds of {{ill|Gekkō Shrine|ja|月洲神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gekkō Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135490862|1=Tsukisu Shrine}} in {{ill|Minamishimamachi|ja|南嶋町|comment=I know this is not a translation but it is actually true and I think this might be not a real location|1=Sakai|2=ja|3=堺市|qid=Q193428|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamishimamachi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * Namimachi (abolished) Note: Not open to the public. In Kita-Tabori-chō, Sakai City – inside the compound of {{ill|Sugawara Shrine|ja|菅原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara Shrine|qq=}} at Nan-dōgahama * Kai (Kitakai) (suspended) Note: Not open to the public. == Abolished Futon Taiko == Due to air raids during the {{ill|World War II|en|World War II|qid=Q362|lt=Second World War|1=World War II}} and subsequent carrier shortages, many taiko platforms were abolished. At its peak in the old city district, there were roughly 30 district groups’ worth of futon taiko—comparable to the total number currently in Sakai City. (Old City District) {{ill|Sugawara Shrine|ja|菅原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara Shrine|qq=}} * Kitajima * Kitahama * Kunigahama * Shakurimono * Yadohama * Ueda * Namimatsu * Kai/Kitakai * Minatogawa * (Others) {{ill|Kaikō Shrine|ja|開口神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaikō Shrine|1=Aguchi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11655187}} * Ōhama * Kenchū-dai (or Daikumi-dai) * Konya-hama * (Various groups) * Daikōhama (revived in {{ill|1998|en|1998|qid=Q2089|lt=Heisei 10|1=1998}}) * Nakadohama * Nakategawa * Higashidaikō * (Others) {{ill|Katagae Shrine|ja|方違神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katagae Shrine|1=Katamigae Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11504380}} * Kawaramachi * Hanadaguchi {{ill|Gekkō Shrine|ja|月洲神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gekkō Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135490862|1=Tsukisu Shrine}} * Minashima {{ill|Tamorikami Shrine|ja|田守神社|1=Tamorino Shrine|2=ja|3=田守神社|qid=Q135039249|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamorikami Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * Matsuya {{ill|Nonomiya Shrine|en|Nonomiya Shrine|qid=Q501266|lt=Nonomiya Shrine|1=Nonomiya Shrine}} * Nakanoshinden (later transferred to floats; now in Higashimachi) * Hatayama (transferred to floats; now in Hatayama-chō) == Taiko Platforms in Other Regions == (Additional sections follow for taiko platforms in cities such as Izumisano, Ibaraki, Nara, etc.;
